so, the Banach-Tarski paradox is essentially a proof of the fact that infinity times 2 is infinity. A sphere has infinitely many points within it, and infinity is never-ending (so we never run out of points), therefore there are two spheres worth of points in one sphere.

I think the issue is assuming that a shape has an infinite amount of points rather than arbitrarily many but not infinitely many. You can divide a sphere into M points where M is arbitrarily large but still element of N (the number set) but you can not divide it into infinitely many points.

There's no paradox. The fallacy is the incorrect use of infinity. Infinity is the anti-set, so you can't do the same type of operations on them and wonder why the results aren't like typical sets. Instead of proclaiming things are paradoxes, it would be better to develop new practical rules for infinity.

The Banach-Tarski paradox was first published in 1929. It shows that either there is something wrong with the axiom of choice or that we can literally make two balls from one ball in the way described in this video. I think it is more likely the case that there is something wrong with the axiom of choice, but that is just my take on it.

The fact we can do an uncountable amount of choices simultaneously is because of the Axiom of Choice, which allows us to provide a well-ordering relation to a set of indexes "J" with cardinal "|ℝ|" and define a transfinite recursion "g : J-->Sphere" by using a choice function (given by Axiom of Choice). 👀

This entire "paradox" rests on imagining that any number of infinitely small points adds up to a surface. It doesn't even work that way. You need to look at these "points" rather as gradually smaller and smaller areas between orthoganal arcs (as you go to more and more points). As soon as you do this, you are removing actual surface and the paradox breaks down.

This isn't a paradox. It's a 3d Cartesian coordinate system with just enough extra steps to be confusing. You need six points in space to describe three infinite axes. But if you have three points and a center you can draw the lines and get the same effect. That doesn't mean having six points magically gives two graphs. It's just approaching the graph two different ways.

The problem with infinite points is that they overlap unless the points are also infinitely small, smaller than any length, even a plank length, which by definition is also impossible unless there is a smallest size something can be.  Once you have a smallest size that a physical point can be, there can no longer be an infinite number of points on any object with a definable size.
Mathematicians often use the phrase, "as x approaches infinity" or "as x tends to infinity" or other variations.  But just as infinity + 1 is still infinity, x + 1 is no closer to infinity than is x.  The implication is that as the value of x is increased, it will approach infinity, but we know this isn't possible.  Perhaps it should be expressed instead as  "as 1/x approaches 0."

We can, in fact arrive at the same point on a circle from which we started. Here's how: Take a rope of any whole number, say 10, and place it in a perfect circle. Now, if we start from any point, and take unit distances, we'll arrive at the initial point. As for the circumference being irrational, in this case, the radius (or diameter, for that matter) would be an irrational number.

Isn't the basis of the paradox that you can't represent a sphere using points, as they have no width, area, or volume? The points are basically a representation or simulation of the sphere, rather than being the sphere itself. That opens us up to these kinds of paradoxes, where you can create a copy of a representation of the sphere, which still doesn't have a surface area or volume.

I'm learning calculus and I went through a lesson covering infinity. The big thing they highlight is that infinity isn't a number. That's the biggest mistake, issue, source of confusion, issue and misconception about infinity. It isn't a number, and since it's conception in mathematics it was never intended to be. It's a concept. Infinity as a number can't exist within the laws of math.
The way I always describe infinite is "everything all at once". For example, if you were going infinitely fast, it wouldn't be a matter of getting anywhere quickly, you'd just be everywhere all the time. It's an easier concept to understand than a never ending string, because the number of numbers between 0 and 1 isn't a counting of a list of numbers, it's all those numbers all at once.
There's really no paradox. If you accept that there are as many real numbers between 0 and 1 as there are between 0 and 2, you can map [0.0, 0.5) -> [0.0, 1.0) and [0.5, 1.0) -> [1.0, 2.0). Now do the same thing with 'surfaces' consisting of an uncountable infinity of points: it's the same thing, two from one. The paradox is just the counterintuitive behaviour of uncountable infinities.

FOr two sets to be of equal cardinality, there has to be a one-to-one and ON-TO correspondence, every element in set A corresponds to a unique element in set B AND every element of B is covered.  Equivalently, show a one-to-one correspondence of set A to B, and also, a one=to-one correspondence of set B to set A.   And the explanation of the diagonalization argument is also bad.

Something I really like about Banach-Tarski is that it's interesting regardless of whether it's physically valid or not. If it is, cool - we've found a way to violate the conservation of energy. If it isn't, we've successfully described a process, using math, that cannot exist in our universe. Our brains - physical objects themselves - are able to conceive of logically consistent rules of a universe different to our own.
A simple explanation: there is a way to "technically" double something by the fact the sphere "can" include left, right, up, down, and the hotel theory can make it include the poles (by having empty spaces and constantly shifting to fill them in) even when it's been halved. In this way, you can double the sphere. There is no way to currently prove this in real life, no such sphere exists to demonstrate it by hand for eg.

I know this is an old video but I couldn't help but notice a mistake at 8:26. The set of points on a circle is in fact NOT countable. The set you get by so marking off points 1, 2, 3 and so on is actually just a countable subset of points on the circle. A simple example to show this is that you can never get to the point that's diametrically opposite to the starting point, because the distance from it is irrational (r * pi).

Okay, to make the 2nd sphere you don't need to move the pieces that are strings of U's to the D sphere. Just rotate the U sphere, add in the D sphere, now we need to add in poles and center, but we still got starting points, since starting points are ½ the poles, ½ the holes can be filled by starting points, now were left with half the holes of half the poles and a center. We can just apply ∞-1 = ∞ to them and get a complete sphere.
We have to be careful when considering paradoxes. Hilbert's hotel paradox suggest that every single hotel room is booked and that there are infinite amount of rooms. If there are infinite amount of rooms, then by definition every single room cannot be booked but since you assume that they are all booked, then there is a contradiction. Language and logic features these paradoxes and can create weird situations like the hotel paradox.

🥱 This is Banach-Taraski paradox:  ∞ / 2 = ∞  In a hypothetical sphere, there are infinite points, so Banach-Taraski "paradox" takes those infinite points to create 2 spheres because both have infinite points.  A real sphere has finite number of particles. If you applied Banach-Taraski to a real sphere, you'd still get 2 spheres but with a lot of small holes inside them... and each would only weigh half of the original, as you'd expect.

This is actually simple. The truth is this: reality and description of reality are two different things. There are an infinite number of ways to describe the points of the circle's surface, but physically there are not an infinite number of physical points. This is where math and physics separate. The only way to make an infinite number of points on the surface are to make the points themselves infinitely small, meaning they can never be realized
Your whole video is constantly assuming that infinity is a number. There is no "number of natural numbers:" and there is nu number of odd or of even numbers. It is not my intuition that makes that only half of any continuous set of natural number is even .If you pair up the natureal numbers wirh the even numbers in order, you wiil find out that the even number is alwats twice as large as the natural number you pair it with. That will never change.
The doublicating sphere is an example of the difference between math and physics. While the description can be doublicated, not every described point contains matter. The matter of the object is not infinite, only the mathematical description of it is, as the description is an approximation, not an accurate representation. The more you zoom in, the bigger in relation will the distance become between described points which represent something real.

This is possible mathematically because of Huygens principle that states: Every point on a wave front may be considered a source of secondary spherical wave, which spreads out in the forward direction at the speed of light. We can think of it as a process of spherical symmetry forming and breaking. The Axiom of Choice is formed by the wave particle duality of light and matter in the form of electrons acting like a blank canvas that we can interact with.
A sphere has an infinite number of points, so of course there are enough to form another sphere, while still leaving the original with an infinite number of points.  Set theory enables us to be mathematically rigorous about it, but at the end of the day, the paradox is a consequence of mucking around with infinities.   We don't encounter infinite numbers of anything in the real world, so the relevance, I think, remains firmly in the field of mathematics.

I have a question, isn't all infinity uncountable? Why can't you do the same thing you did to numbers between 0 and 1 to digits between 0 and infinity? Take the last digit (the ones) in the first number and change it. Then take the second last digit (the tens) in the second number and change it. Repeat this process forever and you will get a new number. Side note: if the number doesn't have that many digits, then you can count it as 0 (143 = 000000000000143).

It's not a new paradox, it goes at least as far as the ancient Greeks. The paradox of the two runners, a slow one that starts first and a fast one. The fast one reaches the starting point of the slow one but the slow one has moved on, the fast one reaches the point where the slow one was but again the slow one has moved on, so on forever. Although the distance may be very tiny the fast one never catches the slow one, he has always moved farther then the trailing runner.

Slight nitpick in 3:52: there are also infinitely many rational numbers between 0 and 1, but the set of rational numbers Q is countable. Countability is more concerned with whether there exists a bijective function f : N -> S, where N is the set of natural numbers and S is supposedly the set that we claim is countable. In the case of real numbers R, or even irrational numbers R \ Q, such a bijective function cannot exist due to the diagonalisation argument given in 4:35.

The given definition of uncountable isn't exactly correct; we can construct a set with the same cardinality as the integers which while still being "uncountable". Imagine the union of the integers, and the set {1/x | x is an integer}. Clearly, we can map all 2x -> n and 2x+1 -> 1/n showing this new set has the same cardinality as the integers, but we cannot count, for example, from 0 to 1 since there will be an infinite amount of elements (1/2, 1/3, 1/4 ect) between them.

The "illustration" that the real numbers are uncountable, understandably, is misleading. It would suggest that the rational numbers are uncountable, but they are not. You can put them in a 1-1 correspondence with the whole numbers. Of course all this is a bit hard to comprehend without some mathematical experience. You can think of the "reason" that the real numbers are uncountable, as precisely that every decimal represents a real number, which is the diagonal argument proof.

No. You simply assumed Infinity does not obey rules applying to the other numbers. The paradox appears only because of that assumption. When a concept such as infinity assumes many “values”, then there is no real or physical equivalence for it. Only in the thought process…  MATH has mislabeled infinity as a concept applied to numbers. What MATH actually requires, is a concept of undefined number, or unknown value, which can take on only one “value”. Then there will be no paradox…

7:51 "You can subtract any finite number from infinity and still be left with infinity." Go further: you can subtract *an infinity* from infinity and still be left with infinity. Example: take the infinity of natural numbers, and remove all of those that aren't *exactly* divisible by a billion. That's an infinity of numbers: nearly all of the natural numbers. Yet what remains - one billion, two billion, three billion, ... - is an infinite set of numbers, the same size set as you started with.

Great video and great speaker. Yet, Hilbert's hotel rooms paradox can be used against the idea that infinite allows what is impossibile for finite logic. Indeed: before the new guest can enter his/her room, say room 1 or room N, the current guest of room 1 or N must move to room 2 or room N +1. But to do so the current guest of room 2 or room N+2 must move to room 3 or N+3. And so on. Exactly because the rooms are infinite, nobody will ever move. result: the hotel is fully booked as for finite hotels :-)

If the publisher redacted the first A, it would still need all the other words, as the Volume A section would only have definitions for words in Volume A. There wouldn't be any definitions for B words or C words, only for those first Volume A words, because they all start with an A. Amazing video otherwise, however just wanted to point out that technicality. The publisher would still need to publish every single word for there to be definitions to all of them.

The part about non-measurable sets is what makes this paradox philosophically interesting to me. The "pieces" have no well-defined volume — they're not objects our intuition can grab onto. There's a rough analogy to how autostereograms work: the depth information isn't actually in the image in any physical sense, yet your brain extracts a complete, solid-feeling 3D structure from it. In both cases, something real (a sphere / a 3D scene) is being reconstructed from something that technically doesn't contain it.
I think I understand the heart of this apparent paradox. It’s perspective. As long as perspective is preserved, an infinite set remains countable. But the moment you change a perspective, aka starting point, then the infinite set becomes uncountable. I actually believe the is my  theory of everything. If you want to define everything relative to a point, you can. But all possibilities are still coded in the define in this perspective. Wave becomes particle, particle contains all waves when perspective is lost.
Good close-up magicians can do much of the dollar bill pieces without referring to Einstein.  As to infinity, we've changed our views on that.  It used to be posited that an infinite number of chimps typing would eventually produce Webster's dictionary or Shakespeare's body of work.  Now they've changed and say perhaps it would not.  In the same way, they used to say infinite stars were more numerous than grains of sand.  No longer, as in any reality, there would always be far more grains of sand than stars...

You don't understand this result; It only means that by using an infinite set of points represented according to rotations, you can represent the infinite points on two whole spheres, if your rotate those spheres, with the same points of representation. The main idea you do not understand is that in the Banach-Tarski construction you break up a measurable set into non-measurable sets. Measure is, in a certain sense, analogous to volume. You have described two spheres, but their volumes are not measurable anymore.

So the paradox hinges on a contradiction within the rules of construction. If you cannot go LR, then you should not be allowed to have LUR and U, since those are identical points. At that point, any point containing a U cannot contain ANY D (and there same for L and R) which eliminates the paradox entirely, since no rotation of any subset of points will leave us with the super set of the sphere. It's not a paradox, your original set of points of rotation labels each point multiple times due to your rule construction.

There's so many inconsistencies and contradictions with these theories. I'm surprised they've propagated for so long. The application of Hilbert's Hotel to the diagonal argument destroys it and implies a one to one correspondence can be created, making everything 'countable.' Then the whole idea of pulling a point from a circle/sphere and shifting the remaining points is flawed, in that points have no mass/density. Taking a circle's circumference and dividing by infinity to get the length/size of the point yields zero.

Science answers the Banach–Tarski Paradox by acknowledging the distinction between mathematical abstraction and physical reality. The paradox is a valid and interesting result in the context of pure mathematics, but it doesn’t apply to the real, physical world due to the discrete nature of matter, the laws of physics, and the limitations of infinite divisibility. Therefore, while the paradox challenges our understanding of geometry and set theory, it doesn’t pose a problem for the logical consistency of the physical universe.

3 men purchase a hotel room. The clerk says "that will be 30 dollars for the room." The 3 men pay 10 dollars a piece evenly. As the men are headed to their room, the clerk realizes he charged them 5 dollars too much. Instead of trying to divide the 5 dollars for the 3 men, the clerk pockets 2 of the dollars, giving the men 3 dollars back....each man getting one dollar back a piece. Therefore the men only payed 9 dollars a piece for the room now. 3 × 9=27 The clerk only pockets 2 dollars. 27 +2=29 What happened to the other dollar?

Stop using the word "infinity". The lazy 8 symbol in maths means "UNDEFINED", being the abstraction of a parameter the value of which is yet to be provided. UNDEFINED is a parameter not a value. Treating UNDEFINED as a value is behind most fake maths presented as paradoxes, including all forms of Zeno's paradoxes. Paradoxes are sophistry, not maths.  "Infinite" in maths does not have the same general meaning as "infinite" in common langue. So use the right word - UNDEFINED or INDEFINITE are correct words to use for the lazy 8 in maths.

The plural of infinity is infinity. Infinity contains every possible human contrivance of infinity and an infinite contrivances of those contrivances. Trying to define infinity is an infinite task. Infinity does not have a size. Only things contained within infinity have a size or can be counted. Infinity to the power of infinity equals infinity. It’s just dividing a countable infinity by two to get two infinities.  Think of it like instead, having every number on the number line (or, number each of the countably infinite lists of points on a sphere).  Divide the number line into evens and odds.  1. Divide the evens by 2, you’re left with the original number line. 2. Take the odds and add 1. Then divide by 2, and you’re left with a second number line.  Doesn’t seem like anything special to me. Once you start dealing with infinities, you necessarily give up any real world applications.

I think, personally, this is an interesting thought experiment but nothing more. Math is merely a tool humanity has used to describe the universe and the way it works. However, every tool has its limits. We should not take math for math's sake as gospel of how something could work in the physical universe, just as we should not expect the English alphabet to be able to capture every conceivable sound in writing. If something defies a physical law (I.E. that matter cannot be created from nothing or added without the use of energy) then it cannot happen.

See Nicod's critic of Hempel's raven paradox. A sphere is a finite shape, to define it in an infinite way changes its fundamental property. Thus the two are not actually the same thing. See:  1. a = b + 1 2. (a-b)a = (a-b)(b+1) 3. a^2 - ab = ab + a - b^2 - b 4. a^2 - ab - a = ab + a - a - b^2 - b 5. a(a - b - 1) = b(a - b - 1) 6. a = b 7. b + 1 = b 8. Therefore, 1 = 0  There's a reason why we don't divide by zero, because when you start mixing infinity with algebra, all answers become possible. Throw in some logic p -> q's and I can prove to you pigs can fly.

Banach–Tarski isn't a 'paradox' of geometry; it's a bug in the Axiom of Choice. If you move from ZFC to a Bounded Set Theory (BST), the paradox completely vanishes. Banach–Tarski requires non-measurable sets and infinite points to work. In a bounded universe, every set is finite and measurable, and cardinality is strictly conserved. You can't turn one ball into two for the same reason you can't turn 5 marbles into 10 just by moving them around. The paradox is just a Category C 'Correct Absence'—it only exists if you assume the universe is made of infinite totalities.

If you were to try and find words based on how many infinities it took to get there per letter, you get the place of where the letter is in the alphabet to the power of how many letters there are in the word.  Here's what I mean: Let's say you want to find the word "stone". S, being the 19th letter qnd the fifth one you see from right to left, would take 19^5 infinities to get to it. For the whole word done in this way, you get 19^5, 20^4, 15^3, 14^2, and 5^1. That is how many infinities it took to reach each letter.

A short mathematical insight of what's going on:  Rotations and translations actually do preserve the volume. The reason why the paradox works is that you are splitting the sphere in sets that are so weird that they don't have a volume anymore (nor 0 nor positive), so when you move them around there is nothing to preserve, and you can get back sets with higher volume.  If you split the sphere in 5 pieces all of which have a volume, you can't grow the overall volume by moving them around.  All this stuff is part of a very important theory in mathematics called measure theory.

@vsauce Issue with Banach-Tarski or Hilbert's Hotel paradox is that it assumes the speed to be constant for moving in one direction or taking out 1 person and adding another person in the hotel room. Infinity is relative to the speed at which you add or remove objects in a given universe. If I remove a person before another person can be added, persons and rooms become finite. The paradox only exists because we assume that the time required to add a person or room is the same as removing or adding another person or hotel room. Please let me know if I am thinking correctly here.

The thing about the real world is that matter, distance, and just about everything is quantized. There are ultimately a finite amount of atoms in an object. Atoms and subatomic particles are the smallest levels we can reach. It’s not possible to duplicate them due to the conservation of energy. You can split electrons into quarks, leptons, muons, and gluons, but ultimately, there are a finite number of them in an object. There are an infinite number of points in the universe, but matter is the limit of what we can manipulate. You can duplicate points, but you can’t duplicate matter

The Banach–Tarski Paradox is not really a paradox, but rather illustrates why there is a flaw in the logic of modern math based on ZFC axioms.  The Axiom of Infinity coupled with the Axiom of Choice in set theory are assumptions that don't necessarily align with anything in the real world.  So you can can ridiculous logic spewing out as a result.  There is some controversy amongst mathematicians about these axioms that emerged in the early 1900s, Cantor's notion of multiple sizes of infinity (complete nonsense: there is ONE size of algorithmic infinity when properly & strictly defined), etc.

Time is uncountably infinite.  You cannot find a unit of time such that the state of the universe is the same between 2 'points in time' - or that you would not have a point in time between them that meets the same criteria.  Banach-Tarski would indicate that losing time is ok, because you can take a slice of time - and end up with 2 full timelines.  This is the way.  It does not matter where you start/end - whether it is the time continuum - or time bounded by a year, a week, a day, a second, or a nanosecond.  Each is uncountably infinite in length. Doesn't the issue lie with the nature of infinity exactly? The way we define different forms of infinity is not the entirety to it.. Like, let's take the set of all natural numbers > 0, so {1, 2, 3, ...}. It has countably infinite number of elements. Now we shift every value by one, so we have the set of all natural numbers with 0, so {0, 1, 2, ...}. Both sets have countably infinite elements, yet the former is a strict subset of the latter. In the end, I find the practical relevance of infinity in some use cases really questionable, as in the end it's just an abstract notion, not an actual measure or numeric value.

I can beat your sphere of knowledge by one dimension and much, much simpler.  First, assign every letter, digit, space and punctuation mark a unique two-digit number.  Second, write out the complete knowledge of the universe using the two digits in step one as a continuous number.  Third, place a decimal point at the beginning of the number in step two, making it a fraction of the distance from zero to one.  Fourth, using the fraction found in step three, place a mark representing the fractional percent of the whole on a line one unit long.   Finally, that single mark can be decoded to write out the text used in step two.

The problem with creative mathematics is that they simply don’t apply to a universe constrained by *countable* limits. For example, there isn’t an infinite amount of matter or energy in the universe. It’s big, but it’s not bigger than the size of itself.  This is why math paradoxes, while interesting, are essentially the same as philosophical paradoxes and puzzles. That we as humans can envision these things is perhaps more interesting, because we imagine possibilities that exist outside of realistic limitations.

These theories are so highly ridiculous.. Infinity in numbers is simply not ‘real’ in the world of physics. Imagine trying to touch something with your hand. Your hand first has to travel half the distance, and then again half of the remaining distance, and then again half of the remaining distance, etc. In theory this means you would never be able to reach anything to touch it, because you always have to travel half the distance first. Countably infinity is the number of elements of the power set of all finite sequences of digits. Uncountable infinite is then the number of elements of the power set of all countable infinite sequences of digits. And theoretically it should go on like this if there were names for it afterwards (aleph 2, 3 ...). 4:06 -> its not the best illustration tbh because all real numbers of the form 0.00...001 are countable (you just need to count from the biggest to the smaller one : 0.1, 0.01, 0.001, etc.)  In fact, the rational numbers are countable. And they can look like 0.1234543212345432123454321... forever but the sequence of digits need to be periodic. Uncountability comes from the 'non-periodic' numbers, so it's a very fine distinction (it lies at an infinitely small scale)  Sorry for misspelling  Edit : I just saw the proof in your video. Aren't Whole Numbers also uncountably infinite? I mean, you can just take an infinitely long integer, and add (or subtract) in the exact same way as you do with real numbers. You might argue that decimals can go forever, yet an infinite number cannot. However, you need to have the definition of infinity, to get an infinitesimal, thus allowing this "uncountable infinity". Also, you could simply move the decimal point to the left a bunch, and bam! You got an uncountably infinite number of integers.  Also, you're defying Hilbert's Paradox if you disagree with me, since you could just add 1 to infinity, like in that Hotel that is very grand indeed.

Assuming natural numbers are countable, real numbers between 0 and 1 are countable aswell, because you can take a natural number, for example 1 and convert it into 0,1 or 10 and convert it to 0,01 and so on and so on. That shows, that natural numbers and real numbers between 0 and 1 are either both countable or both uncountable. If you combine two random numbers of these types and do that with every number you came up with before (every natural number and every real number between 0 and 1) you would have all real numbers. So if you can count natural numbers, you can also count the rest, and if you can't count any of them, then they are all uncountable.

Paradoxes created by invalid conversions between physical units (length versus area, for example) are really hard for humans to see. BTW countable infinity is an illusion. A leap of faith. Every digital computer is finite, yet able to express everything we know so far about the universe.There is consequentially, no way to prove that the real numbers exist in an entirety. Goedel's incompleteness theorem can be used to persuade this. Cantor's argument is bogus and used to brainwash budding mathematicians with a very well disguised tautology. Anyways, the upshot of all this is that the world is only limited by our imagination. There is always more out there.
The infinite hotel example is flawed imho. There is no empty room for a guest to move into. Guest 1 can only move to room 2 if guest 2 isn't there anymore. But guest 2 can't move to room 3 as that is also occupied. And so on.  If you now say, ok, guest 2 leaves the hotel, so guest 1 can move to room 2. Then guest 3 leaves the hotel and guest 2 comes back in and goes to room 3. An so on ... then there is always one guest outside the hotel - not the solution that was asked for. Or to put it differently: that shifting of all guests one room to the right can only work if there is an _empty_ room at the end. But that is not what was specified for this question.
19:24 this could be possible in the real world IF the universe is infinite.  If the universe is finite, then no, because we're not talking about numbers; we're talking about cardinality.   Arithmatic is not enough to handle infinity. Axioms of set theory act as a sort of DLC to fundamental arithmatic, but does not stand alone. Meaning the Banach Tarski paradox has no utility in a finite universe. Even in an infinite universe, I would speculate its utility would be limited to things that contribute to the universe's infinite nature. the problem with the theory is that you change your reference to solve it. when naming the points you refer to the point in the sphere, as referenced by a starting point and the poles. that is all internal to the sphere so works out OK. when you separate it and start rotating however you are no longer tracking a point on a sphere, but simply rotating your perspective of the reference. it requires an outside reference not dependant on the sphere points, and cannot dictate the properties of individual points by manipulating the whole.   in simplistic terms: a cylinder doesn't become a sphere because you look at the top or bottom, only the viewers perspective of it does

Divide the sphere into uncountable infinite great circles all parallel to each other, with points on each circle defined by angles [0, 2pi). Map each point from [0, pi) under multiplication by 2 to [0, 2pi) and the same for a second sphere for the [pi, 2pi). You can cut into any whole number of pieces. The flaw with this paradox is one of the assumptions being inaccurate to reality. The description for each sphere segment is that each point/ray is found by following a set of directions, and the last direction determines which sphere it belongs to. This neglects the possibility that multiple sets of directions can lead to the same place. If I tell you to go north and then east, the destination will be the same as telling you to go east and then north. This is simple vector addition. To say that (1,0) + (0,1) = (1,1) makes sense, and to say that (0,1) + (1,0) = (1,1) also makes sense, but saying that (1,0) + (0,1) =/= (0,1) + (1,0) does not make sense. That's just denying arithmetic.

The problem with this is that the Banach-Tarski sphere creation process is an entirely made up thing. In real life, there is no such thing as an object that when rotated, it duplicates. There is no such thing as an object that is made entirely out of points that appeared by moving in a certain direction. Left, right, up, down, are all relative. Someone standing on the other side of the sphere while you create it would see the opposite directions. This entire “paradox” is based on a completely made up set of rules and made up objects. You’ve basically made up a fictional world, then pointed out how there is a paradox in that world. Well obviously, you’re the one who created it to be that way.

Isn’t this just a manifestation of symmetries? Somewhat analogous to eigenfunction expansion whereby only half of the modes contribute?  You can always create a larger periodic extension with length 2L (or 2^n for higher dimensions?)? Like how sine and cosine expansions both exist, yet only one of them can be used at a time to mimic boundary conditions, or when both of them are considered, the the series converges to one half the jump discontinuity, ie, each one only contributes one half of the response?  But since both expansions are infinite, both expansions can converge to the total response or shape?  But only one of them can accurately reflect the given real  boundary conditions at a time?
The Banach–Tarski Paradox is a misunderstanding of what a point is in geometry. First a point has no length, width, or height. Second a point is a locality not material object, so you can't take a point away from an object, and neither can you add that point to another object. When moving a object you are moving through points/locality and the set of points are not contained in the object.   The issue with the set theory shown in the video is that points are not part of the object, they are localities in the set universe. The object resides in the set universe where the object is residing. By miss stating that the object contains the points you the get nonsense starting around 13:00 in the video.

I don't know if it's me, but 5:15 makes no sense.  If the list includes every possible number between 0 and 1, and you go diagonally down from one number, it still exists somewhere else on the list. The list is infinite. It contains all numbers that could possibly be expressed, but the number that is created by the diagonal will still be *somewhere* on the list, since all numbers are. It's just not on the *shown* list.  As he said before: infinity is not a number, but it is the size and collection of every number. The point about the infinity of fractions being bigger still doesn't make sense. There are more numbers you can express if you use fractions, but there is still an infinite amount of them.

The fact is, you cannot add a number between zero and one. All you're doing is pushing 1 farther away and changing it to #2. That's where this idea breaks down. You may be trying to call the new number, "0.05" but you can't make it "0.05" just because you want it to be that. Every number you add in between the next number, changes the other number one higher. In other words, 0 to 0.05 is actually, 0 to 1 with what you were earlier calling one, now becoming 2. Yes, we add fractions to measure things, but we still represent them as a fraction between 2 numbers, not as an infinite set all on it's own. We're just keeping the #2 label on a number that's actually not #2 any longer.  That's the way I see it.

24:13 ok this is where me as the pleb says that makes no sense.  the diagonal construction method that comes up with (in this video example) 0.68281...etc, would by the definition of that right side list of all decimal values between zero and one (an infinite group) be somewhere on the list already.  Just simplify the example...say instead the right side is a list of all whole numbers from 1 to <10.  And I make the claim that by picking one of them, and adding +1, I've created a new member of the set, which is clearly not a true claim since if I picked 7 and added +1 to get 8, 8 isn't a value that isn't already in that list.  0.68281...is of course somewhere on this infinite list of decimal values from 0 to 1.
I disagree with the distinctions between countable and uncountable infinities. For both real and integer numbers, there are an infinite amount of slots for numbers to go in, and 10 different numbers that could go in each slot, meaning the amount of real numbers and the amount of integer numbers are both 10^infinity. The diagonal argument doesn’t prove anything since this can essentially be done for whole numbers as well. Take every single whole number and list them in order and you can do the exact same thing. Whether a set is countable or not says nothing about its size. It is impossible to start listing ever integer from infinity to 0, yet it isn’t from 0 to infinity despite it being the exact same set of numbers.
Anyone else think the countable/uncountable proof is bogus? I got a minor in math in school, and this always seemed intuitively wrong. But now I'm thinking straight up countable and uncountable infinity being different is actually shite. The diagonal proof is just fun house mirrors. The number of whole numbers is not countable in a finite amount of time. It's uncountable. They're both uncountable. "Where do you even start?" That's just a quirk of decimals. Reverse it for the whole numbers, "where do you even end?" You can always add another digit. Where do you end with the real numbers between 0 and 1? 1. It just depends on the question you ask. I disagree with the "diagonal paradox." Yes, you can write out every real number (or decimal) between 0 and 1, then create a "new" number by combining digits diagonally, then adding (or subtracting) 1 to each one of them. However, having generated another real number, you have created a number that will occur somewhere in the set of numbers between 0 and one. Thus, this "new" number is not "new," it is an existing number that got tilted. Thus, there will be a pace for it in the set of counting -- or "counted" -- numbers. Remember, you're assigning a counting number to every possible decimal in a bounded-but-infinite set, so it exists somewhere in the "real" numbers set, and thus has a corresponding counting number assigned to it.
Interesting video and a fun watch however there is a relatively basic fatal flaw. In order to remove or quantify every single point on a sphere you would esentially be splitting it up or rather dividing into points. A point has exactly 0 volume, as one is 0 (units) in length, width, and height. Whatever the volume a sphere is, devide it by infinity and the result you get back will be zero. To return to the original volume of the sphere mathmatically, you would simply multiply 0 by infinity, which, whatever way you want to turn it, the result will still be zero. Since backtracking the equation ends with different results, it is not mathmatically sound.  Not trying to ruin the fun or the cool concepts in this video! Super cool stuff

Very inspiring visualazation. Thank you. But where the miracle happens?  It is about 14:30: "after doing this to an uncountably infinite number of starting points". Uh, well, it is hard enough to do it for a countably infinite number of starting points, how could we do it for an uncountably infinite number? Actually, it is not possible unless we admit the validity of "axiom of choice", which was formulated as late as of 1904 by a German logician Ernst Zermelo. Only with this axiom of choice can we do "this to an uncountably infinite number of starting points". Still it is less like "doing", but more like a statement of existence for such a choice of starting points, such that the sphere is a disjunct union of countable point sequences.

I think the disconnect between the mathematical and physical worlds, when it comes to properties of infinity, happens at the quantum level.  The very premise of quantum mechanics is that every physical property can be quantized.  That means every property has a minimum unit size.  If that's true, then there would be no such thing as "uncountable" infinites.  Also, if the universe and it's physical properties are finite and measurable in size (which they appear to be at the moment), then there are no "countable" infinites either.  Of course in both cases the numbers involved are so extreme that our minds have trouble wrapping around them.  So for all intents and purposes, they are "practically" infinite.  But "practically infinite" isn't infinite at all.

But any point "X" in an open n-ball can be taken as the origin since there will always be a scalable distance (less than infinity) between any two points.  That is to say, that for any n-ball where the radius is 0 < r < 1 the "center" will be arbitrary.  That's why, for instance, that the universe "expands" (or contracts) away from (or towards) any point chosen as the "center".  When an n-sphere is projected onto a Euclidian manifold the arbitrary zero point of the sphere maps to infinity, but every other mapping transforms to a distance greater than zero but less than one.  This is one understanding that is necessary to understand cosmology.  All distances in spacetime are greater than zero but less than one, where one is the radius of the spacetime n-sphere.
Excuse what may seem like crazy gobbledegook...   The set of whole, positive, real numbers does not count decimal numbers but this does not change the fact that there is still no defined end or limit of the set, just like there is no defined end or limit to the set of decimal numbers. The actual amount of elements in the set of whole numbers is uncountable, just like the amount of elements in the set of decimal numbers, therefore, what has been named “countable infinity” is surely the same size as “uncountable infinity” as they are both defined as this limit of a divergent set. Put simply, you can't quantify a mathematically undefined limit.    P.S. This is my knowledge as I have come to understand but please challenge me if you disagree as I would love to learn.

The concept of countable infinity doesn't hold up as "finite" by my understanding, because you will never be done counting. There is no finite number of hours you will spend counting because after any amount of hours you will not be done counting because the number of elements to count is infinite. The idea of it being a finite amount would indicate that this is not a form of infinity, but rather any absurdly large number. So it would merely be an imprecise colloquial misuse of the term infinity.  Rather countable infinity (by my understanding) refers to the fact that you can start counting because there are definable elements to count. It consists of real numbers rather than unreal numbers (such as π, which can only be approached but not determined in its entirety).

I used this paradox to solve the Monkeys/Typewriters and Shakespeare puzzle. This video is misleading because it talks about points as if they had size. A sphere is a surface of a certain shape. The animation shows the object created by the extracted points as a copy of the original, but I think they are different because they have different properties. Moving points does not move surface, so the original object keeps it original surface and the new object has zero surface, so it is not a sphere. If you want to create a sphere, you need to move *non-zero* sized patches of surface which you can add up to form a shape of sphere.  Preserving all the properties of an object is crucial. You cannot duplicate the value of your bank account by making an infinite number of zero dollar transfers from one account to the other, you need to transfer a *non-zero" amount.
We can create such a sphere in the real world using light and optics. But the results seem to differ from the purely theoretical model, indicating that the model is incomplete. The resulting spheres would indeed be geometrically identical and have infinite uniquely identified points, but in other aspects they are not identical having changed color or lost intensity depending on the method of duplication used. A similar effect can be achieved with a physical version of the chocolate cutting trick by adjusting the unobserved property of density, making it comparable to the animation where the missing volume is suspiciously added back.  In dealing with infinity we must necessarily limit our observational scope, but we need to be careful in doing so not to introduce uncontrolled variables.
It's a paradox because it gives a result that clashes with common sense... until you realise a loophole.  Although the original ball has a volume--i.e., is  Lebesgue-measurable, the five subsets it is partitioned into do not.  The Axiom of Choice provides for the existence of sets that are not measurable, and it is this same axiom that allows us to partition the original ball.  While Lebesgue measure is invariant under rotations and translations, and the union of two measurable sets is measurable, it is NOT necessarily true that the union of two or more nonmeasurable sets is nonmeasurable.  This is the subtle loophole that makes the Banach-Tarski construction work.  It has nothing to do with physics or the nature of physical reality; rather it is a bit of mathematical prestidigitation.

I feel like I have a counter example to the diagonal argument. Haven't taken a math class since high school, so I clearly know what I'm talking about. What if instead of pairing 1, 2, 3, etc with .50328490 , .485929, etc, you just pair the decimal numbers with the corresponding whole number that is exactly the same but simply all to the left of the decimal? Therefore in the above example, you'd pair 0.50328490... with 503,28,490... etcetera etcetera. Both numbers will extend forever, but one after the decimal point and one before it. That way each one will have a match, and if you do the +1 or -1 trick with each digit to create a new number, that number can still be matched with its counterpart (the exact same as the resulting decimal, all digits in front of the decimal place). Thoughts?

I feel like it's important to mention time in regards to this paradox; or lack there of it. The intuition that you can't add another room onto the hotel if there are already an equal number of people filling the rooms implies two states: one in which there is an infinite number of rooms and one in which there in an infinite number plus one rooms. When you add, it implies a change, which necessitates a rate of change if in time. But if all of this is atemporal, then there is no real "movement," as movement is a rate of change of distance. A problem to understand Tarski paradox: "Assume that the ocean covers more than half of the earth.  Can you prove that there is at least one pair of opposite points such that both points are on the ocean? " The problem seems easy at first:  "If every point of land corresponds to a point in the ocean then at least one point of the ocean will be left over and be forced to match with another point of the ocean." This sounds like common sense, but we have just run into a mathematical problem. We have implied that our points cover some area of the earth's surface. Actually if you treat the sphere as a sum of mathematical points then it becomes impossible to distinguish between each point and make the connections. One of the main rules in setting up this thought experiment is that you don't backtrack, it's counter intuitive to backtrack, it does nothing but cancel out and reduce that which is irreducible.  #Infinity

The problem with doing this in our universe is that quantum mechanics doesn't allow for things to be infinitely small. In order to measure something small, you have to use a high-frequency photon to detect it. But the higher a photon's frequency, the more energy you inject into that thing, which increases your uncertainty of where it is. This is of course the Heisenburg uncertainty principle, but it leads to a consequence for the universe. There is a limit to how small something can be to be detectable in the universe, and the width of that thing is the Planck length. This of course leads to a Planck area and a Planck volume.  Tl;dr, infinitely small points can't exist in our universe, and so you can't apply Banarch-Tarski in the real world. The paradox is solved by the fact that you physically can't do the thing that leads to it.

"I dislike the concept of infinity. Life is too short to spend much time on it. I treat both infinity and zero primarily as functions — useful conceptual tools — rather than as robust numbers or observable entities. Infinity does not break mathematics, but it does strain observation and everyday reasoning. I can point to an apple, but I cannot point to the absence of an apple. You cannot truly have 'an empty glass of milk' in a specific sense; once the glass is empty, it becomes an empty glass of anything. The glass itself functions as a finite unit of measure. My conjecture is that any genuinely useful idea involving infinity can be adequately demonstrated or approximated within a finite framework. Zero serves as a milder illustration of the same underlying issue, though it remains far more practical and less problematic than infinity."
@20:45 wait a minute there!  Subatomic particle creation events are nothing like Banach-Tarski decomposition. The ingoing particles smashed are not getting diced up.  It is spacetime itself that gets topologically altered.  But then since all of spacetime is not involved in a collision event it is not really decomposition of any whole.  Same with cosmic inflation: in the eternal inflation scenarios (or even perhaps Penrose's CCC resp.) spacetime itself is never carved up and reassembled, it just gets self-reproduced (by inflaton fields or by the conformal rescaling, resp.).  Same with cosmic expansion, the vacuum does not decompose and reassemble, it just expands and vacuum fields fill-in, like "as if" stretching a rubber band but not adding any extra tension since new rubber gets created. (Lenny Susskind's TTM lectures cover this briefly.)

6:20 interesting problem I thought of: Are there more positive integer squares than positive integer non-squares? The squares can be perfectly remapped back to countable infinity, but the non-squares cannot, there are simply holes in them. A counterargument is that you can count how many non-squares there are in the gaps between squares and the number of those gaps is itself countable infinity, and each gap can be mapped to a positive integer. Furthermore, there is an infinitely growing number of numbers in each of those gaps, so the total number goes to infinity and diverges. another thought: If you add the number of squares, which is Countable Infinity, to the number of non-squares, which may be more than Countable Infinity, you just get the number of all positive integers, which is Countable Infinity. So a<=b; a+b=a ? Infinities are strange.

But surely this so-called paradox is just an attempt to restate the old chestnut "What is infinity +1?" - a  question to which there is no answer, because infinity is NOT a number or a quantity of any kind.  It is simply a word used to reference the fact that, on account of the circular, self-repeating counting system that we humans have devised (get to 9 and then go back to 1, again and again, as many times as you like), there is NO LIMIT (the literal meaning of the word 'infinity') to the number to which we theoretically could count if we were to count endlessly. So the question "What is inifinity + 1/2/3/...?" is as nonsensical as the question "Where is the point that lies beyond the end of this circle?"  The circle has no end: you can therefore not inquire as to where that end is or what lies beyond it, since it does not exist in the first place.
I think the idea of print values solves this. There has been a conception that there are values that are numerical (say, 1.5, 2.6, 8, etc) but there are also values that do not represent an amount but rather a geometrical shape. The print value here would be the over-arching square which holds all divisions in itself. Even though the amount has changed the geometrical area is the same.  It also showcases that our way of looking at infinity is flawed. It all depends on scale really. There is only an infinite number because our numerical system goes based on numerical value and not print value which stays the same no matter how many times you divide.

I don’t agree. 1+1≠1. It might seem that way with the sphere, but even if all points are identically spaced to the first sphere, and they don’t overlap with the measured points on the first sphere, the first sphere is still a sphere. It contains an infinite number of infinities. It is a group of infinite sets, not a singular infinite set. I would argue that the “two different” spheres already existed co-dependently (due to the definition of a sphere having infinite faces) within the first sphere before being separated. Infinity squared = Infinity, and 1 squared = 1. All you’re doing is taking the square root of infinity and finding it to be infinity, just like the square root of 1 = 1.  In conclusion the only infinity that can exist in reality is a self referential one equal to infinity squared, or true infinity. Any other form of infinity is merely conceptual.

This looks like the chocolate bar problem. It looks like it's real, but isn't because you aren't measuring the loss. While you can conceptually remove things in theory, in reality you are losing mass with each removal. Separating the spheres means you would end up with an identical sphere which is proportionally smaller than the original, because unlike the math question where you get to simply declare something infinite, bodies in reality are finite. If you take all the points of the moon and subtract them, you are left with no moon, not another moon made of the other infinite points. Or it could just be that in reality infinity minus infinity really is just zero, no matter how many infinities you have, since the negation can encompass all. So the paradox might just be an illusion, since subtracting one set of points means you are subtracting all sets of points.

Surely by rotating the parts right or left you are adding to the total "mass" of sphere(s). Each point is made up of directional instructions, and each time you rotate you add an infinite number of that instruction to the "part" being rotated, so that is where the increase in sphere(s) occurs.  Obviously only made possible by being able to "extend" further along the infinite number of instructions. Essentially, it is infinite therefore you can't make it any bigger than it is, you've just pulled more of the infinite amount into your observable example. Thank you so much for this illumination of my mind.  Isn't the paradox already in the concept of matter or space? To begin with, we consider a sphere consisting out of an infinite number of points. The sphere has a surface area, an extension in space, but each point has non. If we add up all points of no size we get the sphere, which has a size. It's like adding uncountable infinit zeros up and get out a specific number: the size of the surface area of the sphere. The Banach–Tarski paradox just turns this initial paradox around: you take out an uncountable infinit number of points (zeros) and (1) nothing is missing an (2) of cause they sum up, to whatever you want to. Because any other sphere has the same number of points, too. The whole parados comes down to the solution of naming the points, so everybody can see it.  You can create the whole universe again out of just one part of it.

How can you say "Infinity plus one equals infinity"?  Infinity takes up all the numbers so there's no place to put your infinity plus one. What you're doing for the sake of your fun is misusing the word "equals," it seems to me. Sometime around fifty years ago, the English-speaking world shifted from saying "Such and such divided by zero equals infinity" to saying "Anything divided by zero is not a number" or sometimes "not-a-number," a newly invented special term for this purpose. It seems to me the linguistic reform needs to go a wee bit further, disallowing your misuse of "equals" and forcing you to say something else, perhaps "Infinity plus one is another infinity." The really interesting thing about Banach Tarski from the perspective of a math major isn't even so much that you are taking one infinite set of points and turning it into two identically infinite set of points, there's lots of simpler examples of that like the infinite hotel and showing that there are the same number of points on a 1 inch line segment as there are on a 2 inch segment.   No, what's really interesting is that it manages to do this without any "stretching" or changing the shape. At every step you're just sliding parts of the original sphere around and turning them. Most of these types of proofs involve stretching or changing the size of the object in question. This one is entirely turning things and sliding them, all the resulting shapes are essentially sphere-like and the same size. Even the poles and the center point are dealt with by just rotating them a notch, no stretching involved.
If we consider all possible movements without backtracking using up, down, left, right but limited to sequences of four movements or fewer, the total number of unique sequences can be calculated by considering the following:  For 1 movement: 4 possibilities (up, down, left, right) For 2 movements: 12 possibilities (4 choices for the first movement, 3 choices for the second movement) For 3 movements: 24 possibilities (4 choices for the first movement, 3 choices for the second movement, 2 choices for the third movement) For 4 movements: 24 possibilities (4 choices for the first movement, 3 choices for the second movement, 2 choices for the third movement, 1 choice for the fourth movement) Therefore, the total number of unique sequences of movements without backtracking for sequences of four movements or fewer is:  4 (1 movement) + 12 (2 movements) + 24 (3 movements) + 24 (4 movements) = 64 unique sequences.
Imagine taking half the air out of a balloon to fill a second identical balloon then reducing the surrounding air pressure in half so that both balloons have the same volume as the first balloon originally did. Isn't that a physical example of the banach-tarski paradox? True, there is only half as much air in each balloon (in terms of mass) compared to the amount of air the first balloon originally had. But lets say the air molecules in the balloons are moving faster (due to absorption of ambient energy as normally occurs when compressed gasses are decompressed such as when inflating a balloon using a compressed gas stored in a metal tank) so that the mass-energy density of the air inside the balloons is the same despite the difference in mass. Or we could reduce the pressure of the air surrounding the balloons by putting it inside the balloons. Would that be a physical example of the banach-tarski paradox?

Excellent presentation!  The statement at about 3:25 needs a little clarification:  He says "Countable means that you can count them, from one element to any other, in a finite amount of time."  That's true ... but easy to misunderstand -- it sounds like he is saying "you can count a countably infinite set in a finite amount of time" ... which, of course, is not true.  The confusion is because of the word "them" in "you can count them".  For clarity (and so that no one watching will misunderstand) he should have said "In a countable set you can count *from any element to any other* in a finite amount of time" ... but you can't count *the set* in a finite amount of time.  And when he says that uncountable infinites are "too big to even count", he means you cannot count *every* element of them, or even *every element* of even some small region of them.  P.S. I feel like 5:53 is reliant upon the fallacious idea that it is even possible to create a number between 0 & 1 whose digits are different from every listed number already in the list of numbers between 0 & 1. Just as you can add one more number, at any time, whose digits are unique, you can always add +1 to the list of whole numbers. The very next proof you use proves that. If the number of even whole numbers (x) is equal to the number of even AND odd whole numbers (y), yet y must also be greater than x because it *contains* x in addition to all odd numbers, then the number of whole numbers can be repeatedly proven to be exponentially larger than is previously understood. Y = y + odd numbers. This makes an infinite set of numbers infinitely larger than the infinite quantity of numbers it is itself defined by. Then again, a set of all real numbers contains a set of all whole numbers. Leaving us with the same infinitely exponential result.

Can't you do this in a much simpler way?  Start with a unit sphere centered about the origin made up of points in the set S.  Consider the xz plane (we will use it to divide the sphere into two halfs). Partition S into two sets A and B according to the following strategy: Add all points with a negative y coordinate to set A. Add all points with a positive y coordinate to set B. Add all points with y = 0 and positive x coordinate to set A. Add all points with y = 0 and negative x coordinate to set B. Add all points with y = 0 and x = 0 to set A.  Convert each point in sets A and B to cylindrical form. Define a new set A' = every point in A with its theta coordinate doubled. Define a new set B' = every point in B with its theta coordinate doubled.   Note that A' and B' now represent complete spheres of the their own, with the exception of the poles of B'. However, we can now use the replacement theorem thingy to fill in the two holes in B' and boom.
This video is so awesome. Of course, ask any mathematician worth their salt and they'll instantly know what's wrong with it. In maths (specifically in measure theory) we have developed a system to essentially know what pieces we can chop up an object in, such that we can rearrange it and put it back together, called a Borel σ-algebra. This would be a set of all the sets we can chop the object up into (or apply a Lebesque measure to) so that we can put it back together and get the same set. The trick here is that the sets used to decompose the sphere's surface are not included in the Borel σ-algebra, and so chopping it up into these sets and putting them back together, mathematically does not have to give us back the same sphere. Of course this is perfectly congruent with physics. In the real world it is impossible to chop up an objects into sets that do not belong into the Borel σ-algebra (it includes A LOT of sets). Very interesting video though. Props!

Volumes cannot be constructed from points (‘in the ordinary sense’… or indeed, at all).  Each point has, by definition zero volume.  No length, width or depth.  (If I really wanted to ‘indicate’ a point on a Cartesian graph, for example, I could use a pencil—as we conventionally do…. but how sharp would my pencil have to be to “mark the point” without ‘overspilling’, as it were, the true size of the point?  I want to leave a graphite mark only where the point ‘really is’ and nowhere else).  I make the parenthetical comment to allow for a think—a point, literally has no dimensions.   Any graphite you put on the page misrepresents it’s non-existence in terms of imagined ‘size’.  The idea that it is ‘at’ (1,1) for example is a convention.  In terms of real volume (requiring extension in an imagined space) it isn’t there or anywhere at all.  Points, having no volume the,selves, cannot be, in larger numbers volumes (in the conventional sense or in any sense at all).
I’m on my 7th watch, currently on second year of systems engineering, and this finally clicked.  Something I found very helpful is knowing that this is pure theory and, with physics as we know them, it is physically not possible to recreate the sphere example. The points would need to be completely sizeless. Sizeless points work for most math stuff and graphs, but physical thing cannot possibly be smaller than the plank length as far as we know. If you tried to do this, you’d eventually run out of space to place the dots as the sphere’s surface is not infinite and the dots themselves take unbelievably small yet existent space on it.  The paradox becomes a lot less exciting once you learn this is just a technicality that isn’t covered by our concept of set theory, but set theory itself is awesome and I genuinely wouldn’t be studying my current career if it wasn’t for this video, years ago this opened the floodgates of wanting to understand this mindblowing stuff

Or you could just multiply both sides by zero   1=2 because multiply by infinity, just like 1x0 = 2x0, divide both sides by zero. Except you can't, just like 1xinfinity doesn't equal 2x infinity, because infinite is the description. Intuitively it only describes that description because we can't visualize those quantities as anything else, but that's true. Infinite + infinite= infinite, just like finite + finite = finite. Far from being a genius and this video is so old that I actually wonder why I'm even writing. But my view on it is that this paradox is incorrect. We assume that the amount of starting and ending points are infinite, but the starting points can't be infinite as they will eventually cover up the entire sphere. You can put it in practice by using larger "dots" as starting points and claim that the same thing would apply with smaller ones regardless of how small they get, it would simply take longer to fill the sphere's surface/volume or however you want to call it. That means that by removing ending points of a set you are actually reducing the amount of final spots, hence never getting them back unless if you put the starting points back in. It's just unfair that this video gets so many millions of views poisoning the thought process of people who will leave it thinking that you can actually remove something from something else and end up with double the amount...

This is possible with data but it doesn't work with physical objects. Each unique point would have to point to a location on the sphere that takes up 0 space on said sphere. How do you point to a location that doesn't exist in space? Remember we are trying to apply this to physical objects so each point must be exist somewhere in space but by definition nothing can exist in space that doesn't take up space however small amount of space that is. Even is we assume that space is infinitely divisible, in order to denote a single point in physical space that point must take up some amount of space otherwise how do we know we are pointing to that specific point and not some other point.   For example imagine two infinitely small points on a normal finite table. Both points take up 0 space and are next to each other. Imagine that you can somehow point to one of the points exactly. Now imagine moving your pointer to the second point. How far would you need to move your pointer for it to reach the second point?

I need a physicist. Speaking on 20:39, he mentions the collision of particles. If we take two protons in an accelerator and collide them, we get a Higgs boson.   1. The Higgs boson is more massive than a proton. 125 times more massive in fact. Does that not break the Banach-Tarsky Paradox? The paradox essentially lets us take one ball and make a second identical ball, almost like cell division. In cell division, the second cell is the same size as it’a parent. So then, how does two protons make one larger Higgs boson when the BTP should theoretically mean 2 protons can become 4 protons of identical nature?  2. Does the Higgs Boson break the law of conservation of mass? How can the production of two things create something more massive than the original without external mass? Is there external mass being added during the collision of protons? Since objects in motion produce mass, is the near light-speed movement of the protons in an accelerator generating more external centrifugal mass in order for the Higgs boson to conform to CoM?
By definition there's no such thing as countable infinity, and your explanation of the concept of countable infinity also inherently cannot happen either... there is no finite period of time in which an infinite amount of sequential numbers can be counted, by the definition of the words finite and infinite. Yes, the set of whole numbers is smaller than the set of real numbers, but that has less than nothing to do with the concept of infinity and solely to do with the fact that the entirety of the set of whole numbers is necessarily contained within the set of real numbers.  So, what I'm getting from this so-called paradox is a simple misunderstanding of the concept of infinity. Yes, breaking down an object of infinite size allows you to reconstitute it into two objects of infinite size, but only because half of infinity is still infinity. Likewise, twice infinity is also still infinity. This isn't a paradox, it's a definitionally true statement on the nature of infinity that functions exactly the way we created the term to function.

I feel like they're getting hung up on the language we use to represent the numbers, and inherently, the concept(s) of infinity.  Akin to Plato's 'Allegory of the Cave', the language of mathematics is simply that, an "allegory", and therefore flawed when attempting to make 1-to-1 comparisons. A hotel is finite, and therefore, a hotel cannot contain an infinite number of rooms... by definition. The sphere has an infinite number of points that can be matched on the opposite end of the sphere (same with a flat 2d circle), however, the moment you begin to 'label' them, you are introducing a finite cage that eliminates the infinite nature, and now you get hung up when that finite nature shines through to encircle the thought experiment with boundaries. Again, a thought experiment is an "allegory" used to represent the actual, but will always fail to precisely match the whole. Just like a word, let's say "Love", will never capture the Truth of Love. The word itself is just a simple string of 4-letters, each one lacking its own inherent meaning.
Addition and subtraction are impossible concepts to mash up with the concept of infinity. It's not that +1 or -1 to infinity is "still infinity", it's more that it's just impossible to do given the concept of infinity. It would be like saying, 'if you add the letter "G" to the number 27 it's still the alphabet' (Where "G" is not a variable placeholder for some number like in algebra). That sentence doesn't make any sense, it's an impossible concept to do just like 'adding' or 'subtracting' from infinity is.  Also, "countable" infinity vs. "uncountable" infinity is a completely nonsensical disparity. Both of those concepts are still "uncountable" in result, though, I could see if the fundamental concept of "uncountable" infinity was just not explained very well in this video maybe. Perhaps the disparity in the two concepts is meant to be more to do with something that is not 'sequential' in nature vs. an infinity that 'is' directly sequential, like numbers vs. something organic that would have to have numbers 'applied' to them un-naturally?
I don't believe in different sizes of infinity.  And I don't buy the diagonalization argument, either.  You can't produce a great many numbers on that list, and the argument should end there.  When you perform a proof by contradiction, it just means that one of your priors is false -- it doesn't mean that you get to pick which of the priors is false, and draw a conclusion from that.  Honestly, I think if you're getting results like the Banach-Tarski Paradox, you should now just keep plowing forwards -- you should treat THAT result like a proof by contradiction, and go back, and find the error in your thinking.  I think that we're thinking about infinity in the wrong way.  The axiom of choice even violates the constructions of probability that build on top of it.  For example, if you have a number line from 0.0... to 1.0..., what is the mathematical probability of picking any one number out from that real number line?  It is precisely zero.  But the axiom of choice says that you can pick something that there is 0.0... probability of choosing.
Interesting way to understand countable vs. uncountable infinity and also maybe divide by 0: there a simple measure to count? The amount of space in the universe is countable, because you can’t divide the Planck length, and it is a definable, finite measurement that you could theoretically count, (if it wasn’t too small to see.) Now, imagine trying to count how much nothing there is in nothing. How do you define that? Do you define nothing as the measurement? Do you define it as the amount of space in nothing and use the Planck length? Do you just say that you can’t divide by zero?  The simple answer is that you could say there’s an infinite amount of nothing in nothing, since the measurement can be as small or big as possible. The simplest and easiest to understand measurement would be a 1 dimensional point in space, where you can pack an infinite amount of in a space. Boom, uncountable infinity because you can’t define how much infinity is in a seemingly finite space. Uncountable infinity can be defined as 0 if you wanna be pedantic about it.
As far as I understand it treating one of those "spheres" like a discrete or "one" thing and adding them together is the mistake. Operating with infinity isn't possible, but assuming it is is what it takes to get that 1= 1+1. There's a much better-known and more easy-to-know flawed equation "proving" 1=2 by starting with   x=x, ×=y, x^2=y^2 [...] 1=2  that also manipulates variables in a way that has no analog in the real world. It's just algebraic in this case.  Like he said, infinity isn't a number, nor is it a value. I like to call it a "tendency" where something tends towards that direction. You don't operate with it.  Perhaps an example would be dividing by something like zero:  1/x [as x tends to 0] = -♾/+♾   negative infinity as x tends to 0 from negative 1  positive infinity as x tends to 0 from positive 1  Do we supposedly have two "values" (infinity isn't/aren't value/s) from one "operation?" No. Even the "operation" of dividing by zero makes no sense. It would be wrong for me to act like it's an operation beyond making a point like this.

To call out a flaw in the reasoning behind the infinite sphere. Taking points out of a three dimensional object (a sphere) isn't taking anything out of the sphere. It's a one dimensional thing on a three dimensional structure. Same argument applies to all of the lines. These are two dimensional things. You can't take a one or two dimensional piece out of a three dimensional object. If you took a three dimensional piece out of the sphere, a piece with length width and depth, then you would actually be taking something from the sphere. At that point, rearranging those pieces would net you only one sphere. It's clever trickery and I like it, but it's still trickery. Abstract math is full of them and often relies on semantics, or eventually saying something that infinitely approaches zero is equivalent to zero like the math trick that claims if you divide 1 by 2, and then the result by 2 again, and on for infinity that it equals 0. Except it doesn't. It's just infinitely close to zero. I still always enjoy listening to mathematicians try to prove these things though.

When u say the uncountable line has more numbers between 0-1 than there are countable infinity numbers, ur clearly saying there’s some end to the countable infinite line of numbers. The only difference is the way we label them. Each .0 we add to the uncountable line-up is the same as saying “ok, well, the COUNTABLE number line IS infinite, but now we’re going to just divide the number line differently, and instead of looking at/counting the countable line in sets of one million, we’re going to look at it/count it in sets of hundred thousands.” It’s literally just the way we label the numbers themselves, it does not in any way make the amount any different. Mathematically this is possible but in the physical world we run into planck scale problems. This problem relies on points that are infinitely small around a sphere but in the "real world" the planck scale may be the limit of how small anything can be.. At present, there is no way to predict "with our current knowledge of physics/math" what's going to happen on distance scales that are smaller than about 10 to the minus 35 meters, nor on timescales that are smaller than about 10 to the minus 43 seconds. These values are set by the fundamental constants that govern our Universe. In the context of General Relativity and quantum physics, we can go no farther than these limits without getting a total breakdown of the equations. 1. The quantity of natural numbers (set A) is smaller than the quantity of real numbers (set B).  2. Therefore, set A is smaller in size than set B.  3. Two sets are equal in size if an individual member of set 1 can have a one-to-one relation with an individual member of set 2  4. Therefore, individual members of set A cannot have a one-to-one relation with individual members of set B  5. Set A and set B are both infinite in size  6. Therefore, set A and set B are equal in size  7. Therefore, individual members of set A can have a one-to-one relation with individual members of set B  8. Therefore, set A and set B are both equal and unequal in size and their individual members both can and cannot have a one-to-one relation with each other  9. This argument obviously ended in a contradiction  10. Arguments that end in a contradiction are false  11. Therefore, this argument is false  12. But all the premises are true and the conclusions follow from it  13. Arguments that have true premises and conclusions that correctly follow from the premises are valid and sound  14. Therefore, this argument is both valid and sound, but also false.
