The diagonal model (around the 5:00 mark) is badly explained. Just because you can create a new decimal by adding or subtracting 1 from every digit doesn't mean you'll run out of "countable" numbers to pair it with; just add 1 to whatever number you decided to end at.  For that matter, there isn't really such a thing as "countable" infinity, since there's always the option to add 1...infinity is, by definition, uncountable (because it has no end--literally, 'in-finite', 'without-end').  The big fallacy of the banach-tarski paradox is exactly what Michael said in the beginning, and then forgot: "infinity" isn't a number, it's a concept. So saying "infinity - 1 = infinity" doesn't make sense, because in order to subtract anything you'd have to have a quantifiable amount to subtract *from*, and infinity is unquantifiable by definition. It's like saying "? - 1"; not only does that make zero sense without an actual number to subtract 1 from, it doesn't make sense algebraically, either. Just saying "but it's infinitely big!" is a cop-out, because infinity isn't a number.  Honestly, I hate things like this--false paradoxes, that are only "confusing" because people don't think them through.
Next issue, there are not the same amount of even numbers as whole numbers. As you said, infinity isn't a number. It's not how much. It's how big. Yes, you can say that you can match up every even number with each whole number and it would go on forever, but you can also match up every even number with 2 whole numbers and go on forever. So I'm not exactly saying that what you said is wrong. Just that it's a paradox only created by a lack of defining characteristics. All it's really saying is that an undefined limit can continue infinitely. Since the limit is undefined, you cannot reasonably compare the measurements. No matter how much you try to compare, you will never get to the end to make an actual comparison. You can compare a trillion even numbers to a trillion whole numbers, and there would still be an infinite amount of information excluded from the comparison. It's not that there are the same amount of even numbers as whole numbers. It's that the comparison is impossible to make. We cannot experience infinity. We are limited to finite time and finite ability. We can't even imagine infinity, because once you think you've imagined it all, there's still infinity left to imagine.

I mean if your adding or subtracting 1 from the infinitesimal, why wouldn't you also do that to the "count" as well.  There is no limit to the integer infinity, and therefore +1 is ALWAYS viable to create an integer pair for any additional infinitesimal. I would argue that the integers are not a true infinity, but an infinite series of 1+1+1+1... therefore only 1 is the true infinity, representing the end of  infinitesimals, everything else is a count of how many times you reached infinity for infinitesimals. Hell, you can even think of the integer as being infinity * infinitesimal = integer, so there is an integer for each infinitesimal, just like 1/integer = infinitesimal.   in my opinion, integer infinity (x=infinity) is just a rotation around the number line to the point of xi^1... the imaginary number line. Therefore the integers are just the infinitesimals for  another infinite series of xi^n where n is our new countable series. An infinite number of possible measurements before the wave iterates to the imaginary number line.

Just a layman's thought: This video seems more an exploration of man made concepts, and the assumptions that come along with those concepts, than the nature of the universe we live in. Mathematicians get so lost in the  self-imposed relevance of these mechanisms that their mind folds in on itself and they lose all points of reference to the outside world- calculating themselves in circles.   Having said that: I acknowledge that these concepts are used, successfully to a degree, in our modeling of the universe; however, we should not lose sight of the fact that these concepts are just mental-scaffolding (meaning it does not have to be fully accurate) that will, hopefully, lead us to further observations that we can then use to better model our surroundings.  In short: Focusing exclusively on the eccentricities of these man-made scaffolds, without some real question that ties to an observation in nature, will do nothing but spin us in circles - resulting in an air of undue mysticism where meaning is entirely subjective and the mathematicians, or physicist, cannot agree on what this meaning really is.    Again, I say all of this as a passing layman. I bring a lot of assumptions to this discussion myself =).

One important remark from a physicist: when applying math in the real world, it is only to make a model. It can be very useful, but a still only a model, that is, it is not reality, just a way for us to measure it and deal with it in ways that we can understand. You can think about Newtons laws for example: they are wrong, since their predictions fails to explains phenomena in real world when dealing with speeds close to light. Actually, they are wrong even at low speeds, but the difference between their results and relativity (theory we believe is right, or, to quote Popper, have not falsified) results are neglicibe for the ways we have to measure its effects, that is, they are right in mathematical descripition, but wrong in physical sense. The point is: Barnach paradox is a strange and, in a certain way, fascinating mathematical thing, but math, at least when applied to reality, is never the "truth", just a way to create descripitions to allow us to deal with reality, so just because it is mathematical consistent and useful does not mean at all that it is really what happens. As Einstein said:"as far as the laws of mathematics refer to reality, they are not certinho, and as far as they are certain, they do not refer to reality.
Uh, that's not a paradox. It's the misunderstanding of how 3 dimensional space functions, concocted by a primitive mind. The number of lines you can draw from center, to outer "perimeter" is infinite, a allowing you to make infinite identical "sea urchin" like copies. This stays true, as long as the lines are hypothetical, and are abstract representations of a line, from point to point, within a 3 dimensional space. Hence why this "paradox" must be demonstrated with animations, and not physical models. Once taken from the hypothetical, and applied to the physical, it fails. If you were to take a REAL, physical, solid spere, and use REAL, physical, solid lines, like string i.e., to draw lines from the center point, to the outer "perimeter," each line you drew would account for 3 dimensional space within the perimeter, according to the strings length x circumference = volume. Thus limiting the number of "sea urchins" you could create, dictated by the total volume of the sphere... Ergo, any reassembly of a solid sphere, using the "sea urchins" created, would vary in volume, depending on the number of "sea urchins" used. Only being equal to the original sphere, if the finite number of "sea urchins" created is used in the reassembly...
The flaw in logic here is fairly simple. Our world is not, in fact, infinite. If it was, motion, for example, would be impossible—you have to pass through an uncountably infinite number of points to get to another point, after all. However, motion happens—because sometimes, the sequence just stops. There is a smallest value in real life, unlike in math—atoms cannot be divided up (well, technically speaking, they can, and so can those things, but quarks can’t; that’s beside the point, though). Hence, we can skip the infinitely small movements needed for actual motion. More to the point, there are not an infinite number of atoms to fill the infinite number of holes created by separating a thing into six sixths of that thing. Putting it back together results in a smaller number of atoms than holes, because there is a finite number of only atoms. The spaces where there is not something is infinitely bigger than the spaces where there is, but the simple fact that everything in our universe takes up space (well, everything we can interact with) makes it so that we don’t need an infinite number of points to fill a space divided into infinitely small sections. Hence, things exist, and there is a limited number of things, and duplication is impossible.

5:43 this argument has baffled me for years across multiple teachers, and I do not think it is correct.  “We have defined it to be different from every number on our list”— but we haven’t done that. Changing a digit doesn’t make a brand new number, it just changes it. If we modify the fifth digit of our fifth number, that number probably shows up somewhere else in our list already. If our list is infinite, it necessarily will.  There are not more numbers between zero and one than there are counting numbers; there are the same amount. If you only look at what’s beyond the decimal point, this is plainly true. We can’t count them in order, but every number between zero and one, if you again ignore the decimal point, corresponds to a counting number. 0.0001 would be 1. 0.009745 would be 9745. Or, if you must be technical, then 0.001 would be 1x10^(infinity-3). [I understand that infinity is not a number, but we have to act like it is in some regards to talk about how many numbers there are.] Eventually, the smallest positive number would be 0.000…01. Then 0.000…02, and so on– this would correspond to the list of counting numbers exactly. As such, there are not more numbers between 0 and 1 than there are whole numbers.  If I am wrong, someone please explain.
What I took away from this: infinity divided by infinity is still infinity, and here’s why:  Uncountable infinity has an infinite amount of numbers in between each whole number while countable infinity has one, the number you are counting from (eg. counting from 1 - 2, the number would be one, it is the number you are counting from and to the next one.) So if there is an infinite amount of numbers from one to two in uncountable infinity and just one in countable infinity, uncountable infinity is an infinite amount bigger than countable infinity. Yet countable infinity is still infinity, so if you divide uncountable infinity by countable infinity, you get infinity.  However, vsauce said that there are the same amount of even numbers than there are odd and even numbers, but it is only half right? You skip a whole number for every other whole number every time so you lose an infinite amount of numbers. But you also lose an infinite amount of numbers with countable infinity compared to uncountable infinity. If you can skip one whole number for every whole number and still get infinity, you can skip an infinite amount of numbers every whole number and get infinity, because countable infinity is still infinity, proving that infinity minus itself is still infinity.

If you have that list of infinite numbers between 0 and 1, and then you change one digit in each of those numbers, you're not creating a number that's not on the list. That number will already be in the list of infinite numbers between 0 and 1.   I say that the amount of whole numbers is exactly equal to the amount of numbers between 0 and1. For any decimal amount, you can remove the decimal and you'll have a new whole number. For every whole number, you can add a decimal to the beginning to get a number between 0 and 1. And whole numbers have the same issue about not knowing where to start. You chose to start with 1, but 1 is right in the middle of all whole numbers. That's like choosing to start with 0.5 when counting the numbers between 0 and 1.  The numbers between 0 and 1 are still uncountable, because even if you start at 0.5, you have the same issue of what's next, but even there it's countable, just downwards instead of upwards. You can count to try to find the smallest number. You will never get to 1 that way, but you will also never get to the last whole number by trying to count whole numbers. You can count between 0 and 1 as 0.1, 0.01, 0.001, 0.0001, and so on infinitely. Although I guess that's more like counting whole numbers like this; 1, 10, 100, 1000, 10000.

hear me out:  there are an infinite number of rooms  "ALL" of the rooms are full  if one guest leaves all the rooms are still full. _____________________________________________  if there are an infinite number of rooms and we say, "They are ALL full". To me, the operative  word here is "ALL". When most people hear the word "ALL" the word itself implies a finite set i.e. there are a certain number of rooms that are countable and there are a definite number of them, not a never ending amount of them, and "ALL" of them are full. It seems more like the premise of the paradox isn't valid. But that's just me.  In reality, there is no end to infinity and there is no limit to the set. So, instead of using the word "ALL" implying that we are including a set with definite parameters, maybe we replace the word with something else that better describes a set with no upper limit.   what would that word be, and how does that word better convey the essence of what infinity is? I feel that given the very nature of what infinity is it cannot be subtracted from, because no matter what is subtracted from it, the set can still include all numbers.
I'm not so sure about this one, no matter how small of the piece of chocolate you are removing from the bar, that piece is still gone from the whole, just like the point that is removed from the circle. You might have the points that are very very close to the circle make it indistinguishable, but that very particular point is still removed. Points have no tangibility which is why they don't matter and the circle is still infinite. If something somehow _did_ have tangibility and was infinite, then by default it already has _infinite_ properties. It would already be an endless source of whatever tangible thing it was. For this reason I have a hard time seeing the point of all the mathematical gymnastics used to try and define why/how it can be infinitely duplicated. Its trying to define what is infinitely definable to begin with.  Its like the greek paradox of Achilles racing the tortoise. The tortoise has a head-start on the track but moves very slow. Achilles will obviously outrun the tortoise but at what point does he pass it on the track? We can keep measuring smaller and smaller getting an infinite amount of numbers to try and find the exact point in which Achilles foot steps in front of the tortoise's. But at the end of the day the only tangible thing is that Achilles _will_ pass the tortoise.

The second number after 0 in "uncountable" infinity is unknowable for the same reason as the last number in "countable" infinity is unknowable. Infinity is the idea that you cannot reach a limit ever. It is by definition "incomplete yet" as it keeps growing forever. In uncountable infinity you can still by the way count (forever) the number of 0's on the left side of 1 while trying to determine the second number after 0. So it's basically the same game rules applied in a different context. Therefore, the idea that some infinities are bigger or smaller than others is BS insofar as it permits to compare two *infinite" sets. Rationally, you should not allow yourself to compare that whose size or boundaries are unknown and unknowable, that's undetermined yet and still growing forever.  Actual infinity as used in set theory is an absurdity because it assumes that an infinite set could be completed with an infinite number of elements. Whereas an infinite set will always remain incomplete, hence undefined, because it can grow forever by definition. Thus, you must in fact wait forever before allowing yourself to compare it to anything else because it can never actually exist in the first place. Assuming that an infinite set could exist is a rational sin. I recommend you read the book called The Case Against Infinity by Kip Sewell.
There are two problems in the given proof of the non-countability of the reals strictly between 0 and 1. The first is that reals which have infinite decimal representations ending in all 9's can also be written as decimals ending in all zeros. Those are the only reals with more than one decimal representation (easily proven). In that case always choose the decimal representation of each real in the countable list as the one ending in all zeros.  The second problem is this. Suppose the first decimal place of the first real on the countable list is x (<= 8) and the nth decimal place of the n real in the countable list is 8 for n = 2,3,... Then by the rule given, the real constructed (as the real not on the countable list) is .x99999..... which might be on the countable list if x < 8 (in its form ending in all zeros, i.e., as .(x+1)000...) and which is equal to 1 if x = 8. Here's one example of a better rule for constructing the real (strictly between 0 and 1) not on the countable list. For n = 1,2,3, ..., if the nth decimal place of the nth real on the countable list is 6, then make 5 the nth decimal place of the constructed real; otherwise, make the nth decimal place of the constructed real 6. Of course,  5 and 6 could be replaced by any two different digits from 0 to 8 . 9 can't be used. This idea clearly works in all bases except 2.

There are a couple of problems with this.  First, they have conflated infinity with totality. Infinity is not totality. But totality is infinity. Infinity is, i.e., the series of all known integers. Totality is, e.g., all real numbers.   Second, it ignores boundary conditions. Totality can be unbounded, in which case it is all inclusive. E.g. all real numbers from negative to positive infinity. Totality can be semi bounded, as in the interval from 0 to infinity or from negative infinity to 0. Totality can be bounded, as in the interval from 0 to 1. It is straightforward to demonstrate graphically that bounded totality is the sum of two equal and opposite bounded intervals centered on any real number.   So during the proof where the assumption is made that one can start at 0 and count in one direction only is incorrect. You must start at negative infinity and work towards positive infinity. Or just add two semi bounded totalities that sum to unbounded totality. You must include the negative part of the circumference. If you do this, you create the equation 1-1=0. Without the negative half of totality, what you are actually doing when you say you can create a new object from nothing is basically saying you can ignore the second term in the following identity 1-1=2-2=0 So that you can pretend that 1=2=0 Which violates the addition identity.

It reminds me of Quoting the Bible to prove that God exists.  That's the problem with comparisons to illustrate a point. It's very easy to understand but that comes at a cost of accuracy because if the two things truely were the same you wouldn't call it a comparison. It's in the very nature of comparisons that the thing you Illustrate is not truely the same thing that you compare it to.  It's extremely easy to miss these minute details because in the beginning you just assume that the thing and what you compare it to is the same for the sake of explanation but forget that in reality it's actually not. And so you end up with results that are simply inaccurate.   I guess we humans HAVE to visualize the concept of Infinity with a comparison of something that is NOT truely infinite because we simply cannot grasp infinity. That's the true problem here. Hi Michael,  something you say doesn’t seem right:  at 5:45 “but since we’ve defined it to be different than all the other numbers...”  “it’s clearly not contained in the list” ...     ...that doesn’t seem right:  how is that an effective method of guaranteeing this modifier creates a unique number in an infinite list?  It surely would for a finite list, but not for an infinite list:  Maybe I am oversimplifying or missing something, but just because you modify “each” decimal, you haven’t actually guaranteed the number is different, because the decimals _themselves_ go on infinitely.     Even when modifying decimals in order, your resulting number _must_ already exist later in this infinite list, because _all_ decimals between 0 & 1 already exist is this infinite list.  Therefore that modifier produces a duplicate of a number, it’s just later in the list - remember this list goes on forever!   ...it’s like a race between a number and a modifier; but there is no finish line, so the modifier can never win!  🙃   So it “feels” like this technique will make a unique number, because our minds naturally operate in finite spaces - similar to the next comment in the video about there being the same amount of even numbers as even +odd numbers, it “feels” wrong just because we don’t usually think in infinite lists. No qualms there, I love that point.   Anyway, would love to hear if I’m missing something etc - not heckling, it just doesn’t seem right.  Love the videos!

And among his favorites were those by Martin Gardner and those by Raymond Smullyan  Here's a nice link about Martin Gardner's puzzles...  http://martin-gardner.org/PuzzleBooks.html?fbclid=IwAR0r296RgBGhi1Ed_afo-C8Vo0UYtb4_WI0PrZoF0NQUpEfnci4rgCUnCxM   and one example: "Two missiles speed directly toward each other, one at 9,000 mph and the other at 21,000 mph. They start 1,317 miles apart. Without using pencil and paper, calculate how far apart they are one minute before they collide."  ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++   My dad was also a big fan of the puzzles of Raymond Smullyan.  http://mesosyn.com/mental1-6.html  example:  Knights and Knaves.  On a fictional island, all inhabitants are either knights, who always tell the truth, or knaves, who always lie. John and Bill are residents of the island of knights and knaves.  Question 1 John says: We are both knaves Who is what?  Question 2 John: If (and only if) Bill is a knave, then I am a knave. Bill: We are of different kinds. Who is who?  Question 3 John and Bill are standing at a fork in the road. You know that one of them is a knight and the other a knave, but you don't know which. You also know that one road leads to Death, and the other leads to Freedom. By asking one yes/no question, can you determine the road to Freedom?
By rotating your {rather small} infinite amount of points you’ve just extracted form the area of the sphere, those points aren’t covering the {rather infinitely} small amount of space they were covering (actually not covering anything, since they are points, with no surface), so you can’t cover the entire surface of the sphere AT ONCE, you can only partially cover it, and change the part that is being covered. Everything can be changed, but nothing can appear (nor desappear). Plus; since they are dots, they have an area of 0 {whatsoever} unit all the time. Then [infinty] X 0 = 0 because no matter what gets multiplied by zero gives you zero. (Yes 0 is « stronger » than infinity, that’s for real). Based on this theory, by « splitting » this sphere in 6 groups of dots, you’d have « enough » dots to form other sphere... But can you? You could actually split this sphere surface in a {much bigger} totally infinite amount of dots since they all have no area (or, for those who don’t understand how this is not working, you add dots series by moving « half » a unit at the time). Then you have 12 sets of dots. BOOM! And you can put any amount of infinity packs of dots in this sphere, ‘cause multiplying whatever kind of infinity by zero {surface/area unit} still gives you zero. Otherly said, even after « taking off » the {infinite} amount of dots sets, you still have an entire sphere, since you’ve tooked of nothing of it, unless, off course, you don’t believe in zero, which isn’t possible. Or can it?

Consider the following proof: Lets say a=b  1. multiplying both sides with a we get             a² = ab 2. subtracting b² from both sides            a² - b² = ab - b² 3. Factorizing           (a-b)(a+b) = b(a-b) 4. dividing both sides by (a-b)            a+b = b 5. and since a = b , it becomes            2b = b  6. Dividing both sides by b            2=1  7. Adding 1 to both sides            3=2 8. and continuing doing step 7 , we get           1=2=3=4=5=6=7 ......  All numbers are equal !  Did you catch the error in the above proof ?  Yes its in step 4 .. you cannot divide by zero ...  Notice that by sneakily introducing an error....  you can go on to prove anything .. create any paradox!    similarly ... whenever you use the term "and then" after infinity (which says ever and ever ... never stopping ) you introduce an error ... and from there you can go on to prove anything that you want.  If you observe carefully... All the paradoxes and absurd proofs relating to infinity always somehow manage to sneakily introduce this error ... of saying "after Infinity" .... It is against the very definition of infinity. and you NOT allowed to do it .. just like you are not allowed to divide by zero (which also like saying 2∞ = ∞ ..cancelling ∞ on both sides ... we get 2=1).  Please note that : the 'Divide by zero' error is the same "after Infinity" error in disguise because The elementary definition of division is keep subtracting the divisor until you no longer can ... which in this case is forever and ever...!

This makes no sense whatsoever. First, in your demonstration, you are adding to the paper to fill the space. Specifically with ink. Using different colors, you are actually adding X amount of ink layers and then separating them. You can't apply this to the blank piece of paper without adding the ink. Secondly, yes, the amount of numbers between 0 and 1 may be infinite; however the distance between them remains the same. You are only breaking the numbers down into fractions. So, any number between say, 2 and 3, is actually still 2 or a part of 2. Halfway between 2 and 3 is not . 5, it's 2.5. Therefore, you can't just take away numbers from between two numbers, of any size, and simply fill it in by stating that there's an infinite amount of numbers between them. To put it simply, we can break numbers down to infinity, but, if you take . 0000001 from 3, you no longer have 3, you have 2.9999999. no matter how far you break down the amount between 2 and 3. Our numbering system can't change physical volume, mass and area. No matter how you do it, you cannot separate one object into two separate objects of the same mass, area and volume as the first. Lastly, the idea expressed using the circle also makes no sense. When you remove one piece, leaving a gap, and the shift any amount of other pieces to fill it in, the last piece that moves leaves a gap of the same size. Even in perpetual motion, at any moment, there is either one gap or one gap open for every piece moved. I don't understand how this theory or paradox had even been considered.

i think youre wrong. its not infinite. i think youre just using sleight of hand. this same process can be done on a rubix cube, and there are a finite number of moves on a rubix cube. its alot, but quantifiable. on a sphere, if your movements are 90 degree rotations, up up up up will be the same position as your start, and is the same as down down down if your starting point is positioned 90 degrees 'down' from the former. this has nothing with to do with infinity. youre dealing with a finite space. your quadrilateral movements have a finite number of distances between 0<x>360. i get what youre trying to say, that there are an infinite number of movements possible between 0 and 1 [degrees] alone.  heres the problem with "infinity" though, and what everyone struggles with. for there to even be an infinity there must be a second infinity capable of determining something being non-finite. i.e. whose to say that the movements of 'up' at 0.000 . . . 'ad infinitum' 1 degrees are even movements at all, and the sphere isnt simply fixed in the same position? without that second 'infinity' capable of determining the sphere having even moved at all, it is finite, and therefore cannot be infinite. and if it can be determined as having moved, it is still finite. because youre using an observable, fixed space (the sphere). if the sphere were presumed, and not known, that'd be another question entirely.  but that, is the paradox. there cannot be one infinity, there must be two.  fun fact: its also how we were able to deduce the impossibility of monotheism

As regards Ian Stewarts dictionary, I feel a bit uncomfortable about that argument because he has a list of endless lists, which isnt really a list!   A list is a sequence where you can reach each element after a finite number of steps,   a list of endless lists doesnt satisfy this, you would need to somehow rejig that as one list, eg by scanning back and forth to rejig as one list.   and by continually augmenting with missed out points, you wont be able to get all the points on the sphere, because at each stage you have only done a countable number of countable sets, and a countable union of countable sets is countable. The problem with these paradoxes is infinity. It has no “real” meaning. Having an equal number of even numbers as there are all integers isn’t really meaningful. These paradoxes only seem valid because of the sequential nature of the solution. The same (false) reasoning applies to the Zeno paradox (you can never reach a defined point because you always have to travel half the distance to get there, and there are an infinite number of halfway points).  The paradox of identical copies is an extension of the even/integer numbers problem. You can divide the set of integers into equal numbers of anything. And of course you can do the same with uncountable real numbers. Whatever numbers exist between 0 and 1 is identical in number (even if uncountable) as between 1 and 2, or between 0 and 0.1, or between 0.001 and 0.002.  IMHO, trying to do anything with or to understand infinities is futile, like trying to calculate the circumference of an irregular area like a country. It only makes sense if you define your smallest unit (how many paces for a human to circumnavigate a country, say) - which can be done in physics with the Planck length. Mathematically, infinities obviously do exist; but cannot be operated on as numbers because they aren’t numbers. Indeed, they are probably a special class of non-numbers. In fact, even the notion of comparing different types of infinities is meaningless. Just because the infinity of real numbers is “bigger” than the infinity of integers doesn’t mean that R>I. The definition of infinity means there is no number, so even though they are technically the same, it doesn’t make sense even to say so!
This is nonsense. Consider Hilbert’s hotel rather as a ladder, infinitely tall. The holes on one station are the infinite rooms. The holes on the other stanchion are the infinite guests. But, Hilbert claimed that “all the rooms were full” and that is what sinks this whole idea of shifting the guests. The statement that “all the rooms were full” specifies as an initial structural aspect of the paradox (if it can even be called that). The guests can’t be shifted in the rooms because at any given point in a time of consideration, as per the definition of the paradox by Hilbert himself, “all the rooms are full”. Some manner of appeal to the “elasticity” of the number of rooms cannot be made without the simultaneous appeal to the same with regard to the number of guests, i.e., the maintenance of the condition that “all the rooms are full” (all the time, infinitely). This is a sophomoric scenario and certainly beneath a man such as Hilbert.   The example then of the circle changes nothing and as an analogy to the hotel, it ignores that there is a definitively defined relationship between the rooms and the guests of the hotel. If the problem is considered in the context of the complete or fully specified relationship between rooms and guests, the rooms must remain full out into infinity during that and any consideration so no guests can be shifted in their rooms.   Launching propositions from the erroneous pairings of concepts can only result in a contradiction which material reality does not permit any more than the realm of abstraction, e.g., we cannot appeal to truths to formulate a position which denies the existence of truth.
The problem is that when counting points on the sphere, even though we are dealing with countable infinity, I don’t think you’d ever be finished counting points and thus ready to move on to the next step- the rotations. Sure you could arbitrarily decide you’re done counting points, but you would never end up with the fully reconstructed object after the rotations, you would always be missing some infinitely small piece. The way Michael was able to explain an idea which is so much bigger than the tools we have to explain it is pretty cool, but that’s the discrepancy as well. The idea has been communicated, but no mathematical equation was truly completed in this video. Also, it’s perfectly fine to think about zooming in further and further, approaching infinite zoom to find the smallest point possible, the closest thing to a true singular point, but you would soon reach quark levels of size. Then what, split the quark? It’s still not anywhere near the true meaning of a theoretical singular point. I don’t know anything about these things, God particles or whatever they’re called, but let’s assume eventually you’d reach a particle so small that it’s the absolute smallest thing possible, and nothing smaller can exist. Not a matter of what we know or don’t know, but like actually for real nothing can be smaller. If that’s the case, then we are no longer dealing with an infinity of any kind. If there is a fundamental particle, then the entire concept of infinite singular points making up an object completely evaporates. It may be a number greater than any supercomputer could count up to within a million years, but it would certainly not be infinite.

The diagonal proof seems just like highlighting a crappy organizational problem with a visualization of all numbers.  You take the impossible premise of 'exhausting' the infinity of whole numbers.  That in itself isn't bad, impossible premises can still be useful (imaginary numbers.) Then you iterate each digit place of one side but not the other and get a new number. But if you do the same to each digit place in integers you also end up with a new number, so you didn't actually exhaust it.  There is an infinity of numbers to be mined from both sides this way even after infinity is exhausted.  All its doing by playing favorites with the real-numbers side is create the illusion that they are different.  Treating two identical things differently makes them look differently, but that's only because you treated them differently. It seems like its just creating illusions of logic by disproportionately applying impossible concepts. Its just highlighting the idea of exhausting infinity is nonsense.  1 is essentially 00000(infinite zeroes)001, just like exactly 0.1 has infinite zeroes after (this is pure math not measurement, so forget sig figs).  They're mirror images.  It just seems like we're all crazy, and treating a nonsensical mathematical paradigm with no practical use as though its a breakthrough instead of insane babble. Logical consistency != sanity

im pretty confident that this isnt really a paradox, because no material like this does or ever could exist, and even if it did, we wouldnt be able to work on it in order to bring this "paradox" to fruition.  the entire premise is that you name infinitely many points on the sphere, and then extract those points in order to form a second sphere, but the issue is that a 'point' is not a 'thing'. any sphere we could possibly create would have a finite amount of atoms, and those atoms would have a finite amount of protons and neutrons and electrons, and each of those would be made of a finite amount of quarks, and those of strings, and so on and so forth. i suppose that you could say that 'strings', whatever the hell those even are, are made of infinitely many things somewhere down the absurdity line of matter, but that would then disprove the planck distance, and the planck time, and so thoroughly destroy our current understanding of reality that we would have to throw the whole field out and start over (yes, the entirety of science).  anyways, even if you wanted to pack infinitely many particles of whatever variety into a finite space (already a tall order, considering that our current understanding of the universe is that it contains a finite amount of those particles), that wouldnt do you much good for making a second sphere, since it would just suck all matter in the observable universe from its point of view into itself (infinite particles=infinite mass=infinite gravity).   i think you would genuinely have better luck trying to coax reality into forking over some free matter, something reality is famous for not really wanting to, than trying to pull infinite points out of a sphere.

I thought up a similar concept once.. let's say you want to get to 0 from a certain number.. if you take any number (it's easier with a round number) and divide it by 2 and then take the resulting number and also divide it by 2 and do this pattern continually, the number will keep getting smaller but never quite reach 0... So for instance 100/2=50.. 50/2=25.. 25/2=12.5.. 12.5/2= 6.25.. 6.25/2=3.125...... you can keep dividing like this and get down to a very small decimal like .00000000000001... But even if you divide that by 2 it's yet another number, not 0....  Another way to think about it.. let's say you want to get from one room to another but the room is 10 feet away... But for every step you take towards the destination room you shrink proportionally by half of your size .So if you take a big step of 3 feet, now there's only 7 feet left... However, now you shink proportionally and by half of your size so you shink by half but you yourself are the same dimensions just smaller. Now you take another step with the same amount of space but now because you're smaller, now the distance covered in your same step is only 1.5 feet. Now the rooms only 5.5 feet away. But but because you will shink like the every time, you'll never quite make it to the next room... Eventually after millennia you maybe travel 9.99999999999 FT and be next to the threshold of the next room. Similarly to the first way I explained it, this time you'll keep getting closer to the room, technically you'll constantly be making progress, but you'll never quite make it.... Here's food for thought as to what infinity really is, and yet we will never understand it from beginning to end, because no such thing ever existed but is merely a concept of our linear thinking minds.
I don’t see how the way you’ve labeled the points on the sphere labels every point exactly once with no repeats…   For example, your list includes both the points “U” and “LUR”, which are the same point (since going left and then right cancels out).   In fact, any pairs of left/right or up/down in a name can be paired up and then removed. For example, if you have a point with a name that contains 27 ups, 23 downs, 19 rights, and 26 lefts (arranged in ANY order, such as ULRLRDDULDURRLD…) well, you can just notice that you have 4 more ups than downs, and 7 more lefts than rights. This point is equivalent to the point UUUULLLLLLL.   So, you’re basically labeling every point in the sphere multiple times. It’s no surprise at all then that you can take this set which includes the entire sphere multiple times and decompose it into multiple copies of the sphere!   Really, it seems like the correct way to label each point is to give it a coordinate, (x, y), where X tells you how many lefts or rights (depending on if it’s positive or negative) the point has, and Y tells you how many ups or downs you have. Then, the point LUR and U would both have the position (0, 1).   Second, the fact that you have to repeat this process for an uncountable number of starting points makes me wonder what the point of doing all of this rotation is. Because… the total sphere has an uncountable number of points… so wouldn’t the set of just the starting points be equal to the sphere? You’re basically saying, create a set that contains every point on the sphere. But that’s not the entirety of the sphere until we rotate each of those points every possible sequence of rotations. Again, it seems like you’re double counting. The set of starting points alone is a copy of the sphere, is it not?

Therefore, you are missing a small part and which can be covered of course, but it would make the shape smaller. Unless you zoom in on the duplicated version, you will not get the same thing; in other words, they are congruent but not equal. Another thing, for the part about the duplicated sphere, since one point can never be the same as another one but can countably and uncountably replace the one taken out of the equation, you will not be able to duplicate the same shape of the same measurements unless you squeeze the new duplicate. Even then, it will be smaller than the original shape. This is like taking a cube out of a cube, you'll have an irregularly shaped cube after you've taken it out; however, if you squish the original cube which effectively replaces the cube taken out with other finite infinite points, you will get the same shape but not of the same measure.
There is, in my humble opinion, a large hole in this proof. I will try to explain it 3 times.  Short:  we have to give a name to all points on the sphere. So we have to give uncountable infinite names. The procedure you suggest gives only  names to countable infinite points. You have therefore named exactly 0 % of the points on the sphere! And that is really not enough.  A variation. We have to give uncountable infinite names. We can give the points that are reached from the first starting point: 1DD, 1UDL, 1RDR etc. We can give the points that are reached from the second starting point: 2DD, 2UDL, 2RDR etc. etc. etc. Each name consists of a number followed by a combination of the letters U, D, L and R. These names are countable and therefore useless for the proof.  Somewhat longer:  you take one point on the sphere, but does not give it a name. Let us call this point “starting point 1”. Then you give a name to all the points you can reach from starting point 1 using U, D, L and R. Now you have given a name to countable infinite points. So there remain uncountable infinite points that must get a name. Now you take an unnamed point, let us call it “starting point 2”. You use the same procedure to give a  name to countable infinite points and find that there are uncountable infinite points name left to give a name.  And so on and so on. How many times you repeat the procedure, you are always left with uncountable infinite points which have still no name. I was amazed to hear you say “ After doing this for an uncountably infinite number of times...”. That is as if you want us to find uncountable infinity in the sequence 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, … etc. . Really.  The only way to save this proof is finding a way to number ALL points on the sphere, and I’m afraid I cannot help you there. It seems impossible to me.  Please correct me when I’m wrong.

This is a great video but this is really just a 3D geometric extension of the much simpler Zeno's paradox.  All this shows - in my humble opinion - is that the universe is obviously discrete.  Space and time are quantized, just as energy and matter are quantized.  If you accept that basic assumption, all this makes sense. Infinity just isn't "real" - in the numeric sense.  I mean, come on, you can't square a circle either. It's the same problem.     In other words, and I know this seems weird, but we are clearly doing math wrong.  We are using numbers incorrectly.  Everyone knows that there there are two types of numbers - and two types of math.  Cardinal numbers and Ordinal numbers.  These are different, and the corresponding arithmetic is different.   For example, if I ask you to tell me what (ordinal) number comes after 6, you'd say 7.  But what if I asked you to tell me what comes "after" a dozen?  13? or 24?  That's because cardinal numbers (a "dozen") are not the same as ordinal numbers.    Well, we have the same problem here, but with a THIRD type of number - dimensional numbers.  We all use ONE dimension numbers, like a number line, and try like heck to extend them to 2,3 or even 4 dimensions to solve geometric problems.  But even a High School graduate knows about COMPLEX number (A+Bi).  We call them "imaginary", but they are actually just TWO dimensional numbers.  Yes, complex numbers are really just two-dimensional "numbers".  And, if you take a Computer Graphics course, you will learn about THREE dimensional numbers (Or 4 dimensional depending how you define them), called "Quaternions".   And guess what - you can even go to FOUR (eight) dimensional numbers - called Octonions.    Look - I'm not smart enough to do any of this.  But I really think the Mad Hatter was on the right track.  If you use Octonions combined with discreate space-time (like at the Planck scale), you can solve the secrets of the universe.

Any discussion about the absolute length of a line in a vector graphic is invalid. Only the length relative to the other lengths, and/or some arbitrary initial starting size. Because counting natural numbers, you have a definite starting point: 1. According to our decimal number system you can easily increase from that starting point. You increase each cypher from 1 to 2 to 3 up to 9. Then you switch the 9 back to a 0, add another cypher to the left of it and start there with a 1. Then you count up the first cypher again from 0 to 9. When you want to count one higher you simply increase the cypher to the left of it by one. Or, if there is no cypher yet, you create a new one.  The uncountable infinity in my eyes simply is uncountable because you lack this starting point. You literally would have to write an infinite or never ending amount of zeros before you can write a 1. If we simply set a new way to count real numbers, which would be a mirror of the natural numbers. We could count it. This may just all be hypothetical. For instance, we have philosophical paradoxes like if you walk a foot you can divide a foot in half, divide that in half etc. to an infinity of half steps would fit between your first step. Seemingly this leads to a lot of strange paradoxes- how can you move an infinite amount of space, or take two steps and be infinite times two etc.   But it seems to be more of a trick of our language and mental processing. Quntum physics has shown that time and space is granular and not infinite. So if you measure down to a small enough scale you eventually get a distance that cannot be divided into anything smaller. There is actually a limit to how many times you can divide that step in two- based on some absolute concept of size. Time itself would limit space- space would limit time in the real world. It would all loop in on itself like the globe.   Let's use speed as an example: theoretically you could keep going infinitely faster and then all kinds of paradoxes from that. But in the real world- matter increases in size as it gets faster relative to an observer. Once you get to the speed of light you would have to get infinitely larger- which is impossible. There is a universal speed limit of sorts. All the studies are showing time, space, speed etc. have limitations even if they are unimaginably large numbers for us. It would seem infinity exists as a mathematical principle but maybe not as a part of the natural world around us. That would correct for most if not all of the paradoxes relating to infinity. The hotel has a limitation on its size- at least based on the distance that light can travel and get back to us in a given time. Infinity would only exist in our minds- we could never observe an infinitely large hotel- so it could never exist for us- thus the paradox could not manifest in reality even if we had a magick wand to create anything we wanted in front of us.   You can simply say "this is larger than I can observe" It can't be larger than your observable universe plus one. That is adding a completely hypothetical number that can't exist in real life.

As for the circle, it only works because you don't define the size of the point. There is only an infinity number of sizeless points, and sizeless points don't actually exist. Or I should say they exist the same way that nothing exist, because they are in fact nothing. And that makes sense. No matter how much of nothing you take, that nothing remains the same size. It only changes if you add something to it, like volume. You can't take away nothing. You can only add something to it. If I have taken nothing, then I haven't taken anything. And yes, I'm using word play. Wordplay akin to the mathplay at work here. To say there's infinite points of nothing is kind of meaningless. Let's say we use 1 planck length as a measurement for a point. You can still fathom half a planck length. Whether it's possible or not, the concept is logical. A planck length is a whole, and a half is a part of that whole. There's nothing smaller, but we're talking about infinity and numbers, and there's always smaller numbers. So while half a planck length isn't possible, the number for half a planck length is possible. I say this because there could be more numbers on a circle than planck length points. If you were to use planck lengths to measure the radius and circumference, and let's say the radius was 2, then the circumference would be some fraction of a planck length, ~12.6. And the point I was trying to get at was that if you move 2 planck lengths every time, then you'll still hit the same planck length points over and over again, but really I think what I've shown is that if there is a minimum size possible, then there are no circles. Because what would be the minimum number of planck lengths necessary for a circle? 3 would e a triangle. 4 would be a square. 5 a pentagon. 6 a hexagon, and so on for infinity. The only true circle would have to have an infinite number of planck lengths.

To anyone confused about the term "countable" to describe an infinite set:  It's a rather unfortunately misleading term, and it does not mean that you can literally count through all of the infinitely many members of the set in a finite amount of time, but it does make sense when you consider the mathematical definition:  an infinite set S is said to be 'countable' if there exists a bijection (a 1-1 and onto function) from the set of natural numbers to S.  There's a few things to unpack in that definition:  The 'natural numbers' are simply the set of positive integers: {1, 2, 3, ... }  (sometimes it also includes 0, that's just down to convention and how you choose to define them)  A 'function' between sets is a mapping from one set to another, e.g. let A = {1, 2, 3} and B = {X, Y, Z}, one possible function from A to B is: 1 -> X, 2 -> Y, 3 -> Z (where -> should be read as 'maps to').    A function is '1-1' if each member of the 'domain' (the set being mapped FROM) maps to a unique member of the 'codomain' (the set being mapped TO).  In the previous example, A is the domain and B the codomain.  That function is 1-1 because no two members of A map to the same member of B.  The function 1 -> X, 2 -> Y, 3 -> X is not 1-1 because both 1 and 3 map to X.    A function is 'onto' if every member of the codomain is mapped to by at least one member of the domain.  The original example function is onto because all members of B (X, Y, Z) are mapped to.  The second example is not onto because nothing maps to Z.  Finally a 'bijection' is just another word for 'a function that is both 1-1 and onto'.    With that out of the way, it is not too difficult to see why we call such infinite sets "countable".  If a set S is countable, then we can map to each member of S with a unique natural number, or in other words we can index the members of S as S = {S1, S2, S3, ... }.  The terms 'listable' and 'denumerable' are sometimes also used, equivalent to 'countable'.  It is not immediately obvious though why we even bother making such a distinction, but as the video shows, there do exist some infinite sets that are 'uncountable' (simply meaning 'not countable', i.e. there exists no such bijection between the natural numbers and the infinite set).

For a more in depth explanation to seeing why it's not so much of a paradox:  First, you have to consider the question "how do you measure length? area? volume?"  Just consider the real number line, how do you measure how long any subset of the real line?  Well, what mathematicians do is create a function that takes a subset of the real line, and spits out either a number greater than or =0, or infinity if it is infinitely long. This function is mathematically known as a MEASURE, or more specifically a LEBESGUE MEASURE.  So the length of the open interval (0,1) is 1, the length of the whole real line is infinity, and the length of the Union (0,1)U(2,3) is 2.  Now, this measure satisfies the following properties:  1: the measure of the empty set is 0 2: the measure of any set is positive, 0, or infinity 3: if a set A is a subset of B, then the corresponding length of A must be less than or equal to B  4: moving a set around (translation) does not change its measure 5: the length of the closed interval [0,1] is 1 6: The length of the union of a bunch of sets A1,A2,A3... is less than or equal to the individual lengths added together. 7: The length of the union of a bunch of sets B1,B2,B3... that do not overlap is exactly equal to the individual lengths added together.  (When I say a bunch of sets, it just means a finite or countably infinite amount of sets)  All of these properties give us the length we expect of assigning a length to every subset of the real line.  However is this definition well defined?  It turns out that the answer is NO. There are sets that give contradictions (assigning a set multiple lengths for example). Such sets are called "Non measurable sets" as there is no meaningful way to assign length to them.  The Banach Tarski paradox employs non measurable sets in the construction of the pieces.   However it turns out that any set which is non measurable is really artificial. You can't make any of these sets in real life. If you exclude the non measurable sets the Lebesgue measure works perfectly fine.  Mathematically, it is not possible to make any of these sets without a powerful tool known as the AXIOM OF CHOICE (known as AC) This axiom allows mathematicians to make countable and uncountably infinite choices at the same time. Similarly the Banach Tarski paradox uses the AC to construct the sets.
Just some random thoughts; and not an "expert" by any means, but I would say we are confusing infinity with infinite here.  Infinity is that which has unmetricable.  In order to start to define a metric, we need a property that is fundamentally different from the infinity, but which relies on the existence of the idea of something that cannot be measured.  I would propose the metric of "other".  Infinity is unmetricable, so its existence is not dependent on any metric.  If we create an "other" then when we examine its properties; the "other" examines itself internally and it finds no metric within; it examines itself externally and finds no other metric outside of itself either.  Its existence depends on being a metric, so it is not the Infinity.  The point is a mathematical fundamental metric that is sufficiently something to be able to measure with, but fundamentally composed of no metric within.  We have exhausted all the possible properties that a point has inherently, now we simply need to create a space that we can examine what properties are emergent from a collection of points.  A space that inherits the properties of a point, but that is composed of "all" points.  I would define that new metric space as the Infinite.  One might argue why not just create another point? I would say that the property of spatial awareness is not developed enough to justify the idea of a plane or a line or something sufficiently metriced.  We need to examine the properties of a collection of points as their own collection.  Why not start with nothing instead? Because nothing is a metric that depend on another metric for its meaning.  If a metric is to have any real definition, it must be in relation to that which cannot be defined by a metric.  Infinity, unmetricable: {  }  Other; { 1 }, the shape of the point doesnt matter, so this is just simpler to represent a point.  Infinite; {11111... }, a collection of all points.  The property of, "always continues" because we have a definable unit metric to start with.  I would think of the point, tethered to infinity; like a balloon tied to a rock.  The infinite is a bunch of balloons tied to the same rock.  No point shares the same placement as another point - the concept of position is undefined, and since point is a unit metric, then no 2 points can share the same "placement" in the infinite space.
That comparison between real numbers and whole numbers was very biased. What was done to the real numbers (where one digit of every number was changed), could also be done to the whole numbers, if you think of every place left of the ‘highest’ digit of the number to be an endless string of zeros. Not only that, but because we know that there can only be one decimal point in any one number, you could simply remove that decimal point to not only convert every real number between zero and one into a whole number, but every real number between zero and infinity. Whoever made that diagonal approach clearly didn’t even attempt to work backwards.  Furthermore, saying that all real numbers are ‘uncountable’ is simply false. All you would need to do is determine some shortest distance and multiply it by the number of whole numbers there are. For instance,  you can easily create an infinitesimally small number with the formula:  1 - (1/2 + 1/4 + 1/8 + 1/16... )  Then just multiply THAT by every whole number there is to infinity.   The same effect can be achieved by placing a decimal point at the infinity-th place of every whole number, adding zeros where you need to.  This may be unfeasible on a sheet of paper, but so is ‘traditionally’ counting to infinity.  Comparing the size of infinities is redundant anyway, any extra ‘numbers’ you create - be it through power sets or the diagonal method or whatever - could simply be counted first, before continuing with the already existing whole numbers, or you could count them as negative numbers ( -1 then -2 then -3) then just add the lowest whole number you reached’s absolute value to get an equivalent amount of whole numbers when you’re done creating new values. Essentially making all the whole numbers start at zero and count up.  Another method would be to convert whatever string of ‘things’ you were counting into a whole number with the base of however many states each index can have, choosing the highest index if it is dynamic, then converting that number into base-10. That would make a list of whole numbers that was equal to or greater that the original infinity in cardinality. With the power set example, just convert each power set into binary, any whole number not included would be a low and any number included would be a high in that whole numbers place, then just convert those binary numbers into base-10.
I've come up with a way to do this in 8 pieces by optimizing a method I saw elsewhere. Here is my construction: Let's choose two axes that pass through the centre of the ball and denote the 180° rotation around one of them as flip and the 120° rotations around the other one as left and right rotations. The angle between the axes should be chosen in such a way that no irreducible combination of the three rotations is no turn. (Here I call a combination irreducible if it doesn't have two same rotations or a left and a right rotation in a row.) Now let's choose a point in each orbit on the sphere of the ball as the starting point. For each point in an orbit, let's write down the way to get there from the starting point using the rotations, prepend it with infinite alternating flips and right rotations in order to avoid problems with the starting points and reduce it. Now we have a way to get there from an infinite path. The last move in this path determines the set out of three initial sets it belongs to. Now the sphere, except for the countably many poles, is dissected into three sets: flip, rotate left and rotate right. Flipping swaps the flip points and other points and left and right rotations change the sets in a cycle. Let's unite the right and left sets into one set. Now we have two equal sets, one of which can be dissected into two rotated copies of it. Let's dissect the ball into these three (including the poles) parts by putting each point in the set the corresponding point on the sphere (the end of the radius that passes through it) is in and putting the centre into the set of poles (though it doesn't matter where we put it). Now we can duplicate a part that is equal to the flip part; let's do that 4 times and combine the resulting parts to form three balls without the poles and poles of one ball as an additional part; one of these parts also includes the centre. Let's rotate one of the balls without poles in a way that it now has all the poles by choosing an axis that is not pole and an angle that does not turn any poles to other poles because the set of angles that do is countable and take the poles and a point as the centre out. Now we have two balls and some extra points. Let's put the points that end up there after repeated application of the construction so far into the poles part (or any other part if you move the balls to make that part not move).
Acceptance of this paradox is required in the Mathematics community, because rejecting it means that a large portion of this edifice of "proofs" built upon prior "proofs", under construction since Cantor, would have to be torn down and cast into the waste bin. This is unthinkable to those who grew up thinking that the Axiom of Choice wasn't rotten timber, but that doesn't mean it's not the correct thing to do. And refusal to face the unthinkable, is a psychological barrier, not a mathematical one.  Unthinkable, because it would mean your dissertation was was... worthless. Unthinkable because it would mean your whole career has been wasted mining iron pyrite (fool's gold). Unthinkable by those at the top of the pyramid of academic reputation. But again, these are all psychological barriers, NOT mathematical ones. Belief doesn't transmute iron sulfide into gold. Belief can certainly DELAY the collapse of a fiat currency, or a Ponzi scheme, but only temporarily.  Properly understood, the Banach-Tarski Paradox isn't a paradox at all, it is a simple case of Reductio ad Absurdum, or proof by contradiction. In this view, it demonstrates that Cantor was WRONG.  If you think that belief doesn't create a powerful fake reality that can vanish into thin air, when doubt begins to infect the community of believers, then you don't understand hyperinflation of fiat currency, you don't remember the "dot-com" bust, or the financial crisis of 2007-2008. Mathematicians, unlike physicists, don't have a Mother Nature to lay waste to their theories with "inconvenient" experimental results. Mathematicians, unlike theorists in physics, rely ENTIRELY on reputation and consensus. This consensus-only system, unique to Mathematics, works well MOST of the time ...until it fails. And then there is no mechanism to correct it. Thank you for sharing but I think this video misses the whole point about "infinity".  1) I can't agree with part one on "countable" infinity because I think that's just a misconception. When you say "we used up all the whole numbers", what you mean is "we reached the end of the finite list of numbers" but that list is NOT finite. Thus you will never end up "lacking" numbers. This is the same as saying "even numbers" vs "integers". You are just shifting your group definition. I think you grasp the idea when you say "lets start with 0.000 and add 0, when should we start ? We can't start because there is always one left" -> exact same with the whole numbers. You cannot end, you cannot say they are countable. You could say they are "iterable", but they are not countable because the "infinite set of numbers" has no length, because length means "head" and "tail". But "tail" means "the end" and there is no end to an "infinite amount". That's like saying "start by counting the length of an infinite universe, in kilometers, then <blablabla>". You could start at some point and count : 1 km, 2 km, 3 km, 4 km, ... But you will never say 'hey i've reached the end, let's proceed with the next instruction"  2) Hilbert's hotel : You can't fill an infinite number of rooms. Imagine you have N the number of rooms = infinity. Now M. Smith comes to you and books a room. So you now N = infinity - 1. This notation is absurd but let's forget it for now. Next person would do N = infinity - 2. So having no rooms left would mean that when you count 1, then 2, then 3, then 4, ... you would at some point reach "infinity" but that's not how infinity work ! Because that would mean that there is a finite amount of numbers and that you reached the end. That would mean that there is a x value that solves x+1=Infinity. That does not exist, by definition.  3) "After an infinite number of those" -> not possible, because that implies that at some point, he would say "i arrived at the finite boundary of the infinite and will now proceed to AB".  4) Again, we cannot color all points because it is infinite ... Feels like this video misses the whole point of "infinite". This is a fairly difficult concept for us to grasp as it does not appear to us in nature, but saying "oh we were iterating to the infinite and reached its finite boundary" is just a problem of definition.  Would be happy to debate around this and be proven wrong.

I always laughed about Cantors Diagonal Argument since I was in pre-school. One can make similar claims with a chain of simple whole numbers and call it a horizontal argument. Whatever numbers you write down, you can always find a number with a noncontained permutation of digits in comparison to what has already been counted. The joke becomes even bigger when one makes Cantors diagonal argument using binary depiction of numbers. In the end one can find a constellation which claims 0.11111... and 1.0 not being the same number and there being at least one number in between them not listed yet. While simply counting whole numbers, all non-contained numbers are showed to the end of the chain, while Cantots list shoves them to the bottom of the list. Some mathematicians interpret Cantors diagonal argument as a different kind of 'openness' of the two 'actucally' infinite sets(such a thing existing is unprovable and every analysis mathematician will agree). A well ordering of whole numbers and real numbers is like the difference between having a line [0,1[ and having an open square with the right and top side being 'open', trying to find a way to bijectively map the point 1 of the first to the right/top border of the later. It is simply a different kind of the 'openness'-property. Similar applies to Hilberts Hotel by induction, where you can theoretically shove every resident to the next room to make place for a new customer, but you will always have at least one person walking on the floor, knocking on the next guys door and sending him off to the next room... the process never finishes, until the last person in the chain finds an empty room. Either one has at least one person for all eternity running to the next room and knocking there to send the next guy walking, or one has an infinite amount of persons doing that simultaneously, which amounts to the same time spent on the floor in total. One might as well just send the new guy to the end of the chain of rooms, always walking, never arriving.  Similar question arises with spacetime when one asks wether or not space is actually infinitely expanded, while the only direction we can measure in is vectors pointing towards the future, while entropy and reversibility arguments indicate there is no end to the time vectors... just our way to measure metrics breaks down. Even in a collapsing star, no single particle ever actually reaches a singular point in space.
Actually, operating under the assumption that every guest is paired with a room, and always will be, then there will never be an empty room. Every time you add a room, you would need to add a new, separate guest, no guest will ever change rooms because no room would ever be empty. Guest 1 would always be in Room 1, as the infinities are linked together, as said by the rule. Meaning that if you kept going and you added a room, let’s say Room 100,000,000, you would also need to add a guest to fill that room, being Guest 100,000,000. So if someone walks into the hotel, they would not be able to get a room simply because if a room is added, another guest would also need to be added. In other words, you wouldn’t be able to move guest 1 to room 2, as room 2 and every room after will already be filled, no room would be empty for any guest to be moved to. Every guest will have a neighbor in ascending order, and thus wouldn’t be able to fill the room that is already filled. So all in all, if someone walked into the hotel and requested a room, it would be completely filled up and would have no room for that one guest.  Just for further clarification, take this example. Guest A walks into the hotel and requests to be in Room 1 (occupied by Guest 1). Since both infinities have already been laid out in order, and Guest A isn’t apart of either Infinity, he cannot be assigned a room, as Guest 1 will always be inside Room 1 and Guest 2 will always be in Room 2. If Guest 1 were able to be moved to Room 2, then the infinities would be unlinked and then the underlying rule, being that every room is occupied by a guest, would be broken and the whole scenario would be completely different, because then the rule would have to be changed from “for every room is a guest” to “not all rooms have to have a guest” and thus would completely change the entire situation, evidence being that room 1 would be left vacant, so the rule of every room being filled would be false. Guest A cannot live in Room 1, because only Guest 1 will. Acting on the original rule, you cannot possibly add a room that is vacant whatsoever, with every room, is a guest to match it, and if a vacant room is created and Guest A is assigned to it, then the rule of “every room has a guest” would be inherently false, since you created a room that didn’t have a guest immediately to match it.  I hope this made sense, let me know if there are any areas I should clarify.

Is Aristotle's wheel paradox a physical example of the banach-tarski paradox? https://youtu.be/mW-0bZwoGwQ  https://youtu.be/mrVg9GM5h7Q  Imagine taking a large wheel and small wheel which are concentric as you usually see in demonstrations of aristotle's wheel paradox and wrapping a length of string around each of their circumferences. The lengths of string match their circumferences exactly. One end of each string is anchored to the wheels where the radial mark on each wheel is at while the other end is free. Now, imagine unrolling the strings by pulling the free ends horizontally from the wheels with the wheel's center remaining in the same spot. The strings will unroll to their full lengths with one longer than the other. Next, roll the strings back onto the wheels and then hold the free ends of the strings and roll the wheel off the strings instead of the strings off the wheel. What do you expect will happen then? Will the wheel be able to be unrolled the full length of both strings or will it only be able to be unrolled to the length of the shorter string? I expect that only the shorter string will be able to be unrolled from the wheel while the longer string will remain partially unrolled. This seems paradoxical to me because unrolling the strings from the wheel is equivalent to unrolling the wheel from the strings (isn't it?). So their lengths should be the same regardless of whether we unroll the strings from the wheel or unroll the wheels from the strings. But the results are NOT the same both ways. When we unroll the wheel from the strings while holding the free ends of the strings taut then the two lengths of unrolled string will be the same when the wheel is unrolled as far as it will go. But when the wheel's center remains stationary and the strings unrolled from the wheel then the strings will be unrolled to different lengths. Is this not paradoxical? On the one hand it is what I would expect but on the other hand it is paradoxical because I expect that unrolling the wheel from the string is equivalent to unrolling the string from the wheel and so should yield the same results either way. Yet these two alternative methods of unrolling the string yield different results. Or so I predict. I also predict that while the wheels will complete a full revolution at the same time when fully unrolling both strings from the wheel (when the wheel's center is held stationary) the wheel does NOT complete a full revolution for both strings at the same time when the wheel is unrolled from strings (whose free ends are held stationary). This is another paradox. I still need to physically demonstrate all this to be sure my predictions are correct but this is what I predict will happen.

All this Anomalies arise because we have assigned a symbol (∞) and a name (infinity) to it .. which tricks our brain into thinking that its some very very very huge number .. its not a number ... its just an idea... a thought concept of saying never ever ... so all statements like ... lets say we have infinite 'number' of so and so is incorrect .. ... its not a number .. and treating it like a number gives rise to all these paradoxes ... its just incorrect to say ... infinite 'number ' !!! You begin by explaining this same thing in the beginning and proceed to make a false statement that it is size... Its not even size ... Its just an idea... A thought experiment ...  When describing countable infinity (which again is absolutely wrong ) you say "its still finite... will take more than the life of the universe etc.. " .. its not .. you can say that about something like Grahams number ... but not about infinity ...   Also you cannot describe infinity without using time … like never or forever .. in an obvious or hidden way.  Like its a point on the number line which ‘never’ comes .. you keep going ‘forever’ and  'ever' .. the moment you stop .. its not infinity… its a promise to never stop and not a place on the number line. You can cleverly mask the time dimension by saying something like its the point in space where parallel lines meet … but we know they ‘never’ do.   So you cannot say imagine a hotel with infinite rooms..you are assuming it to be a very very very large number again .. and if you start with wrong assumptions .. you'll end up with wrong results ... paradoxes and anomalies ...  That hotel will never be complete and will ‘always’ be under construction.. adding more rooms ‘forever’ and ever … only after the hotel is done building can you move on to the next statement that each room has a guest .. but by definition it NEVER does stop building in the first place .. !!  Therefore saying something like bigger infinity or smaller infinity are also meaningless.. that is where all these paradoxes come from .. you are assuming it to be a number ...a quantity ..  a place... a point... a size... or a ‘something’ .. its not  ..  The diagonal proof also starts with a false assumption .. it starts by saying .. lets say you have written down all the real numbers.. and then moves on to the next step .. but by the very definition of Infinity ... you can never finish writing all .. never … ever .. If you could be done writing 'all' then its not infinity  .. how or rather when can you move to the next step?   Even in the dictionary example ... you say you write all AAAAA's "and then" .... you move to B.... there is no "and then" ... the very utterance of those words violates the definition of Infinity!
